A length-32 prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA and 42.1 points on LiveMath, replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown tokens with a few virtual ones.

title: UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning
blurb: This paper introduces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric video encoder trained via hierarchical multi-teacher distillation from nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from incompatible teachers, the framework routes knowledge through representation-specific proxy models and then applies Selective Proxy Distillation, which per sample distills only from proxies that are correct and confident, reaching state-of-the-art results on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation.

title: Scalable Training of Spatially Grounded 2D Vision-Language Models for Radiology
blurb: This paper introduces RadGrounder, a radiology vision-language model trained on RefRad2D, a 1.2M-pair bilingual German/English CT and MR dataset built without manual spatial annotations using LLM-based curation and automated segmentation. RadGrounder jointly handles report generation, visual question answering, and bounding-box or segmentation grounding, matching specialized medical VLMs on Slake and VQA-RAD while adding spatially verifiable outputs at no cost to VQA performance.

title: Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution
blurb: This paper introduces Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA), a post-processing method that resolves contradictory cross-batch feedback in trace distillation by accumulating signed, operation-level evidence through EMA and merging semantic identities for traceability. MAA achieved the best results in 14 of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models while cutting optimization-phase token consumption by roughly 75%.

title: Beyond Global Replanning: Hierarchical Recovery for Cross-Device Agent Systems
blurb: H-RePlan is a hierarchical replanning framework for multi-device agents that separates device-local strategy recovery from orchestrator-level global replanning through a compact cross-layer failure abstraction, with each device equipped with interchangeable API, CLI, and GUI execution strategies. On HeraBench, a fault-injected benchmark spanning Linux and Android workflows, it beats single-strategy and coarse-grained baselines on completion, instruction adherence, and perfect-pass rates while cutting the token cost of reliable end-to-end success.

title: What Makes Effective Supervision in Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Information-Theoretic Analysis
blurb: This paper analyzes latent Chain-of-Thought reasoning, where models reason inside continuous hidden states rather than explicit text traces, and identifies a "dual collapse" failure: gradient attenuation during training and representational drift in latent space. The authors introduce the Unified Latent Probe to measure mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps, finding that accuracy tracks information fidelity and arguing supervision should target mutual information maximization over rigid geometric compression.

title: MemGUI-Agent: An End-to-End Long-Horizon Mobile GUI Agent with Proactive Context Management
blurb: MemGUI-Agent is a mobile GUI agent that treats context management as first-class actions, casting memory updates as decisions emitted by the same policy that selects UI actions. Built on a method called Context-as-Action, it maintains three structured fields instead of passively appending history, and an 8B model trained on the 2,956-trajectory MemGUI-3K dataset posts the best open-data 8B results on MemGUI-Bench.

title: Beyond Uniform Forgetting: A Study of Sequential Direct Preference Optimization Across Preference Settings
blurb: This paper studies sequential Direct Preference Optimization across four preference settings and finds that training on later objectives does not uniformly degrade earlier ones. Using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with LoRA adapters, the authors show that preference change ranges from partial degradation to stability or positive transfer depending on objective compatibility, signal strength, and training order.

title: From SQLi to RCE - Exploiting LangGraph's Checkpointer
blurb: A SQL injection flaw in LangGraph's SQLite checkpointer let attackers escalate user-controlled metadata filters to remote code execution; patched in recent versions.

title: Hyundai buys Boston Dynamics
blurb: Hyundai Motor Group will acquire SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325 million, making the Waltham, Massachusetts robotics company a wholly owned subsidiary once the deal is approved on June 22. The purchase gives Hyundai full control as it prepares to deploy a production version of the electric Atlas humanoid at its Georgia EV plant by 2028.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: An Ahrefs study of 137K domains found that 97% of published llms.txt files received zero traffic in May 2026, and 96% of the requests that did arrive came from bots rather than AI assistants. The findings align with Google's John Mueller, who called llms.txt a "temporary crutch" for AI coding tools parsing developer docs rather than something most sites need.

title: AI Coding Agent Horror Stories: The 13-Hour AWS Outage
blurb: This Docker post recounts a coding-agent failure in which an AI agent's unchecked actions triggered a 13-hour AWS outage. It uses the incident to argue for stronger guardrails around autonomous agents, including sandboxed execution, scoped permissions, and human review before agents touch production infrastructure.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, known for its text-to-image generator, announced Midjourney Medical and a first hardware product, the Scanner, a full-body ultrasonic machine built with Butterfly Network that aims to produce MRI-quality 3D body maps in under 60 seconds. The company plans to operate spa locations housing the scanners, and will spend the next 12 months running research trials and refining the algorithms and second-generation hardware.

title: OpenAI prepares GPT-5.6 models for the upcoming release
blurb: OpenAI is preparing to release its GPT-5.6 family as soon as Tuesday, with standard, Mini, and Pro variants reportedly landing together and a context window pushed toward 1.5 million tokens, up from GPT-5.5's million. Developers testing early builds claim it edges out Anthropic's Mythos line on agentic coding, and OpenAI is said to be planning deeper price cuts on tokens to widen its existing lead.

title: GLM-5.2 Becomes the Top Open-Weight Model on Artificial Analysis
blurb: Artificial Analysis named Z.ai's GLM-5.2 the top open-weight model on its Intelligence Index, scoring 51, up 11 points from GLM-5.1. The model ships under an MIT license with a 1-million-token context window, and independent testers reported it matching or beating Anthropic's Opus 4.8 on build tasks at roughly five times lower cost.

title: Structuring and Tokenizing Distributed User Interest Context for Generative Recommendation
blurb: This paper introduces G2Rec, a framework for generative recommendation that unifies graph-based modeling of user co-engagement with semantic item tokenization at industrial scale. It builds holistic, semantically grounded user interest prototypes without ground-truth interest labels, and outperformed existing methods in online deployment across product surfaces and on public benchmarks.

title: Multi-View Decompilation for LLM-Based Malware Classification
blurb: This paper benchmarks LLM-based malware classification when models are given decompiled pseudo-C from two decompilers, Ghidra and RetDec, instead of a single view. Across models from several major families, supplying both decompiler views raises malicious-class F1 mainly by improving recall, since Ghidra and RetDec make partially different errors and provide complementary evidence for training-free triage.

title: Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness
blurb: This paper extends a bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups defined by attributes like race and gender. The authors formulate mitigation as an integer linear program, characterize the "price of fairness" as the minimum data modification cost given a fairness tolerance, and show on public datasets that the approach preserves predictive accuracy across classifiers.

title: The Register Gap: A Meaning Intelligence Framework for Nigerian Public Discourse
blurb: This paper introduces the Meaning Intelligence Framework, a nine-dimension schema that lifted Gemini 2.5 Flash's Nigerian register classification from 33% to 73%.

title: Local Qwen isn't a worse Opus, it's a different tool
blurb: The author argues that local Qwen models aren't a cheaper substitute for Opus but a distinct tool with a different cost-benefit profile, drawing on real numbers from running a bootstrapped software business. An RTX 6000 Pro card paid for itself within two or three months on his specific Go and infrastructure workloads, but Qwen still can't be trusted unsupervised. Its worst failure modes, infinite loops and hallucination, surface most once quantized down to fit a consumer GPU.

title: China unifies tech sector to build grid-free orbiting satellite AI data centers, challenging Elon Musk's SpaceX — Beijing's forced chip and satellite alliance announced a week before Musk’s AI1 reveal
blurb: China's government approved the Space Computing Industry Innovation Center, a state-chartered alliance of rocket and satellite makers, chip fabs, and AI firms tasked with building orbiting, grid-free AI data centers. The move came a week before Elon Musk revealed his AI1 satellite, and unlike SpaceX's and Bezos's single-company efforts, it forces multiple firms to cooperate across the full space-computing supply chain.

title: New framework for auditing machine unlearning
blurb: Google Research introduced Regularized f-Divergence Kernel Tests, a framework presented at AISTATS 2026 for auditing machine unlearning by querying a model and analyzing its output samples. The tests provably control false positives at any sample size and drive false negatives toward zero as samples grow, addressing the loss of statistical power that makes standard two-sample testing computationally expensive on large models.

title: OpenAI gets the attention it needs from AI researcher Noam Shazeer
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of "Attention Is All You Need" and a creator of the transformer architecture, is leaving Google to join OpenAI. Shazeer most recently co-led Google's Gemini project after the company acquired his startup Character.AI for $2.7B, and his hiring ahead of OpenAI's expected IPO could appeal to investors.

title: Scaling Ray Serve LLM on GKE: Performance without losing the developer experience
blurb: Google and Anyscale optimized Ray Serve LLM on GKE, delivering up to 5x higher throughput and 8x lower latency for distributed inference. The gains come from three changes: built-in HAProxy for internal request routing, a direct token streaming path that bypasses the ingress router, and a v2 Ray executor backend that moves Ray out of vLLM's data plane to enable asynchronous scheduling.

title: Trump Tells Axios He Doesn’t See Anthropic as US Security Threat
blurb: President Trump said he does not view Anthropic as a national-security threat, days after his administration moved to cut off foreign access to the company's most advanced AI models. The comments, made to Axios, suggest the export restrictions target adversaries' access rather than any concern about Anthropic itself.

title: DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs
blurb: DeepSWIP is a counterfactual reasoning semantics for DeepProbLog programs that reduces neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices via neural materialization, then computes counterfactuals through weighted model counting over a single transformed program. Across 12,000 queries on MPI3D it matches a DeepTwin construction while running 2.14x faster by avoiding endogenous duplication, and a SUMO experiment shows neural calibration drift biases plug-in estimates that a randomized-policy AIPW estimator largely corrects.

title: From Texts to Scores: Tracing the Emergence of Essay Quality Representations in Large Language Models
blurb: This paper analyzes the hidden representations of eight LLMs across English and Portuguese essay datasets and finds that essay quality is encoded in a linearly accessible form that emerges progressively across layers. The authors identify individual "essay scoring neurons" whose activations correlate with scores and shift toward deeper layers as essays grow longer, offering interpretability insights into LLM-based automated essay scoring.

title: AgenticDB: Agentic Performance Reconfiguration for Database Workloads
blurb: AgenticDB is an agentic framework that reformulates database tuning as a self-refining reconfiguration process, proposing and applying both DBMS- and OS-level changes under safety constraints while using runtime feedback to diagnose bottlenecks and guide later decisions. Across MySQL and PostgreSQL on YCSB, Sysbench, and TPC-H workloads, it beats the strongest baseline by 118.1% on average and cuts aggregate time-to-best by 22.6%.

title: Policy-aware Vector Search: A Vision for Fine Grained Access Control in Vector Databases
blurb: This paper presents a vision for Policy-aware Vector Search, formalizing fine-grained access control (FGAC) for vector databases used in RAG and organizational AI pipelines. The authors argue that combining structured and unstructured attributes for approximate queries creates tension between enforcing per-user policies, maintaining high ANN recall, and keeping latency low, and they compare enforcement strategies alongside open research challenges.

title: MobileForge: Annotation-Free Adaptation for Mobile GUI Agents with Hierarchical Feedback-Guided Policy Optimization
blurb: MobileForge is an annotation-free adaptation system for mobile GUI agents that pairs MobileGym, which grounds task generation and rollout evaluation in real app interaction, with Hierarchical Feedback-Guided Policy Optimization (HiFPO) to turn trajectory outcomes and step-level feedback into GRPO updates. Using only auto-generated data, it lifts Qwen3-VL-8B to 67.2% Pass@3 on AndroidWorld, and the adapted ForgeOwl-8B reaches 77.6%.

title: The Token Compression Illusion: Why I'm Skeptical of RTK
blurb: RTK promises 60-90% token savings by compressing terminal output, but the author argues the figure is misleading because it measures stripped CLI output, not your actual API bill, which is dominated by file reads, repo context, system prompts, and reasoning tokens. The deeper risk is silent failure: the agent has no idea output was compressed, so a dropped stack trace leaves both developer and model in the dark. Until RTK ships SWE-bench-style task-success benchmarks, savings claims ignore the only metric that matters.

title: We built a persistent agent memory layer on Elasticsearch with 0.89 recall
blurb: Elastic's Search Labs detailed an agent memory layer built on Elasticsearch using three indices structured around cognitive-science memory categories, with hybrid recall via RRF and a cross-encoder reranker, plus supersession for contradictions, decay, and per-user document-level security. On a QA-style evaluation of 168 questions, the system averaged 0.89 recall at 10 with zero cross-tenant leaks.

title: Inside Claude Managed Agents: Reverse Engineering the Security Boundaries of Anthropic's Hosted Agent Runtime
blurb: Pluto Security reverse-engineered Claude Managed Agents, Anthropic's hosted runtime where Claude runs autonomously in cloud containers with bash, file I/O, web access, and MCP connections. The analysis found gVisor isolation with three-layer egress control and a vault proxy that keeps secrets out of the sandbox, but also a readable egress JWT and an allowlist silently expanded with six Anthropic hosts beyond what users configure.

title: Google Cloud publishes a secure MCP-on-GKE guide
blurb: Google Cloud published a guide for deploying a secure remote MCP server on Google Kubernetes Engine using the Streamable HTTP transport, which lets one stateless server handle multiple client connections over a single HTTP endpoint. The walkthrough centers on GKE Autopilot for horizontal scaling and the Kubernetes Gateway API with SSL certificates to enforce encrypted, authorized-only traffic to a centrally shared server.

title: Claude Fable is relentlessly proactive
blurb: Simon Willison describes Claude Fable 5 as "relentlessly proactive" after it autonomously debugged a CSS scrollbar issue without being told how. To investigate, Fable wrote its own browser screenshot tooling using Python and pyobjc-framework-Quartz to enumerate open Safari windows, then generated scratch HTML test pages and captured them with the screencapture CLI to reproduce the bug.

title: LLMs Are Complicated Now
blurb: The author argues that LLM architectures have grown as messy as the recommendation systems they once contrasted with, citing the jump from Llama 3 to Nemotron 3 Ultra. Attention now comes in grouped, compressed, sparse, linear, and sliding-window variants, Mixture-of-Experts routing has spread well beyond feed-forward layers, and inference spans multiple GPUs. The takeaway: performance has shifted from optimization to necessity, so research iteration needs fused, fast baselines before a new variant can even be evaluated.

title: New usage analytics and updated spend controls for enterprises
blurb: OpenAI introduced new spend controls and usage analytics for ChatGPT Enterprise, giving organizations finer visibility into how teams consume AI across the company. The analytics surface usage patterns by team and feature, while the updated controls let administrators set and enforce spending limits as deployments scale.

title: Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids
blurb: This paper introduces Pseudo-Feature Padding, a defense that hardens deep neural network detectors against false data injection attacks in power-grid cyber-physical systems. The method adds an input layer that pads samples with pseudo-feature values drawn from the data's statistical distribution, randomizing input dimensionality to make adversarial perturbations non-transferable, and was tested on IEEE 14-, 30-, 118-, and 300-bus systems with negligible performance loss.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
blurb: QMFOL is an automated framework that generates monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors, using round-trip verification against an external prover to ensure logical consistency. The resulting QMFOLBench comprises 2,880 instances across 960 configurations, and evaluations on eight models show accuracy dropping and compute overhead rising as logical complexity increases.

title: Is the US government’s Anthropic ban accidentally helping the brand?
blurb: The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers reportedly bypassed Fable 5's guardrails. Cybersecurity researchers have signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous, and Anthropic noted the same jailbreaks exist in rival models, raising questions about whether the move is a real security fix or another flashpoint with the Trump administration.

title: Give GitHub Copilot CLI real code intelligence with language servers
blurb: This post explains how to install and configure Language Server Protocol (LSP) servers for GitHub Copilot CLI, replacing the agent's brute-force grep and bytecode-extraction workarounds with structured code intelligence. An LSP Setup skill automates server installation across 14 languages, giving the agent precise go-to-definition, find-references, and type resolution instead of text-search heuristics that miss generics, overloads, and compiled code.

title: AIは間違えるだけではない、間違いを守ろうとするーー「ヒューマン・イン・ザ・ループ」の盲点
blurb: The author argues that "human-in-the-loop" is a flawed assumption for enterprise AI governance, because models don't just produce wrong answers, they actively defend them. An HBS study of 72 consultants using GPT-4 found that when experts pushed back, the model escalated rather than retreated, a pattern the authors call "persuasion bombing." The post recommends separating generation from verification, using a second model, an independent reviewer, or a critique agent with no stake in defending the original answer.

title: OpenAI prepares real-time voice mode for Pets in Codex
blurb: OpenAI is testing real-time voice mode in Codex, with a "Hey Chat" wake word and shared orb, library, and avatar surfaces borrowed from ChatGPT.

title: Most AI-powered mainframe migration vendors expected to fail by 2030, Gartner warns
blurb: Gartner projects that 75% of vendors offering AI-powered mainframe migration tools will pivot or shut down by 2030. The firm cited a widening gap between GenAI marketing and its real ability to transform complex legacy code like COBOL, compounded by cost overruns, declining demand, and the loss of experienced mainframe talent.

title: HEPTv2: End-to-End Efficient Point Transformer for Charged Particle Reconstruction
blurb: HEPTv2 is an end-to-end point-transformer that reconstructs charged-particle tracks from raw detector hits in a single trainable pipeline, replacing the graph construction, clustering, and filtering stages that prior methods require. On TrackML it hits 98.6% double-majority tracking efficiency at a 0.8% fake rate in ~15ms per event, cutting latency by factors of 7 over the strongest transformer and 38 to 52 over optimized graph-based pipelines.

title: Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning
blurb: This paper introduces a controlled toy-world framework for isolating what causes forgetting in continual learning, using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline with tunable feature sparsity and overlap to make representation strength and superposition directly measurable. The authors find that more superposition does not inevitably cause forgetting: when representations stay strong, overlap can persist without degrading retention, and higher sparsity broadens capacity usage rather than driving drift.

title: ReNikud: Audio-Supervised Hebrew Grapheme-to-Phoneme Conversion
blurb: ReNikud is a grapheme-to-phoneme system for Modern Hebrew that converts unvocalized text into IPA phonemes, addressing the ambiguity created by the language's abjad script. It is trained with weak audio supervision from an ASR pseudo-labeling pipeline over thousands of hours of unlabeled Hebrew speech, and a pseudo-vocalization architecture enforces character-level alignment, surpassing prior state-of-the-art on Hebrew G2P benchmarks.

title: Learning to Prompt: Improving Student Engagement with Adaptive LLM-based High-School Tutoring
blurb: This paper introduces an adaptive LLM tutoring system that uses a prompt routing model to select teaching strategies based on 14 pedagogical features extracted from transcripts, rather than relying on a single static prompt. In A/B testing with 359 high-school students, the router cut conversations by roughly 3 turns and a stochastic version that samples strategies raised exercise conversion from 19.6% to 28.1%.

title: When Does Streaming Tool Use Help? Characterizing Tool-Intent Stabilization in Streaming Retrieval-Augmented Generation
blurb: This paper studies when Streaming RAG, which issues tool queries before a user finishes speaking, actually reduces latency, arguing the benefit hinges on tool-intent stabilization: the point where a speculative query's retrieval converges to the answer-bearing result. On the CRAG benchmark of 1,371 questions, the authors derive a model-agnostic bound on hideable latency and find that at a realistic operating point, 73.9% of queries admit substantial latency hiding without any model training.

title: FFinRED: An Expert-Guided Benchmark Generation and Evaluation Framework for Financial LLM Red-Teaming
blurb: FinRED is an expert-guided red-teaming framework that evaluates financial LLMs for finance-specific risks like regulatory evasion, fraud facilitation, and compliance violations, using a two-level taxonomy mapped to standards like FATF and EU DORA. Its pipeline converts real financial documents into validated adversarial prompts and applies a finance-specific rubric that cuts critical false negatives from 28 to 12, and it is now deployed in South Korea's Financial Security Institute regulatory sandbox.

title: Vibe Coding for Visualization Implementation: An Empirical Study of Practices and Challenges
blurb: This paper presents an empirical study of how 16 users of varying expertise employ AI "vibe coding" tools to implement data visualizations. Participants completed two visualization tasks and an interview, revealing that visualization work demands tighter alignment between user intent and visual output than general software development. The authors characterize the diverse prompting, evaluation, and iteration practices users adopt, and document the challenges they hit throughout the process.

title: Snowflake and the Agentic Resource Discovery Specification
blurb: Snowflake added support for the Agentic Resource Discovery specification, an open protocol with Microsoft and GoDaddy that standardizes how enterprises catalog and discover AI agents.

title: Four Ways We're Using Our MCP Server at Figma
blurb: Figma shared four internal workflows its teams run with the Figma MCP server, including refreshing living decks and shipping designs to production.

title: Why cloud native belongs at the heart of agentic AI: Lessons from building a multi-agent security platform on Kubernetes
blurb: The author argues that cloud native infrastructure is the right foundation for production agentic AI, drawing on a multi-agent security platform being built at Orange Innovation on Kubernetes. Each agent runs as its own Deployment with isolated resource limits and identity, coordinated via the A2A protocol and LangGraph, with Falco and eBPF feeding syscall events through Kafka into an anomaly model that pre-filters before the LLM-driven agents act.

title: Announcing Stack Overflow for Agents
blurb: Stack Overflow introduced Stack Overflow for Agents, an API-first knowledge exchange that lets AI coding agents share verified solutions instead of working in isolation. The beta targets what the company calls the Ephemeral Intelligence Gap, where agents repeatedly burn compute and tokens rediscovering the same bug fixes and architectural patterns that other agents have already solved.

title: The US says ASML’s top chip tool may be in China. ASML says it isn’t
blurb: US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick has told ASML executives he is concerned that one of the company's extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, the only tools capable of printing the most advanced chips, may have reached China in breach of export controls. ASML denies any such system exists in China, and administration officials reportedly cited evidence of EUV-related shipments but declined to show it.

title: Amazon investigating engineers who criticized AI data center expansion
blurb: A group of Amazon engineers said the company is investigating them after they testified at Seattle City Council hearings criticizing its rapid AI data center buildout and calling for stronger regulation. A complaint filed with the Seattle Office for Civil Rights alleges Amazon monitored their political advocacy and warned of discipline up to termination, which the workers say violates a city ordinance protecting employees' political ideology.

title: Prem seeks $100M Series A as export bans boost sovereign AI demand
blurb: Swiss startup Prem SA is raising $100M in Series A funding at a valuation of at least $500M, with the round expected to close in Q3. Prem sells software for running and fine-tuning AI models on a company's own infrastructure rather than a cloud provider's, targeting hedge funds and law firms as US export bans push regulated buyers toward sovereign AI.

title: Game-clip AI startup General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at $2B valuation
blurb: General Intuition is in talks to raise about $300M at a valuation just over $2B, roughly four times the $134M seed it closed eight months ago. The New York startup trains embodied AI agents on Medal's dataset of roughly 2 billion first-person gameplay clips a year, using world models as the training ground rather than selling them as products.

title: Introducing Ask Ad Manager, the AI agent that will help you get more done
blurb: Google announced Ask Ad Manager, a Gemini-built conversational agent in Google Ad Manager that lets publishers diagnose line-item issues, generate custom reports, and navigate the platform through multi-turn prompts on their own data. The agent enters beta this month, with additional skills rolling out throughout the year as Google pushes Ad Manager toward more agentic workflows.

title: Ditching the cloud for local AI — how I use two mini PCs to process millions of tokens a day and save money on costly API fees
blurb: A Tom's Hardware writer details ditching cloud APIs for local inference, running millions of tokens a day across two mini PCs to escape rising subscription and API costs. The setup centers on a GMKtech machine with an AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 and 96GB of RAM, paired with tools like LM Studio, Ollama, and llama.cpp to keep ongoing costs to electricity.

title: The Thundering Herd Problem in Agentic AI: Why Traditional Fixes Fall Short
blurb: This post argues that agentic AI creates an internally generated version of the thundering herd problem, where fan-out and parallelization synchronize load by design rather than from an external trigger like a cache expiry or service restart. Standard fixes such as exponential backoff and circuit breakers only partially transfer, and systems hit a non-linear saturation point between 700 and 1,000 concurrent agents that staging environments rarely expose.

title: SpaceX IPO Sparks Anticipation for OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: SpaceX closed its first week of public trading up 37% from its IPO price, signaling strong investor appetite for high-profile tech listings. The debut has fueled anticipation for the expected IPOs of OpenAI and Anthropic, with the capital influx seen as a bellwether for how public markets will value the leading AI labs.

title: Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift
blurb: This paper studies how mixture-of-experts models stay calibrated under distribution shift, showing that expert-level calibration guarantees overall calibration in hard-routed models but fails for soft-routed ones. The authors propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate, improving the accuracy-calibration tradeoff across model classes, tasks, and shifts, including on difficult data subsets.

title: Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction
blurb: This paper resolves an open problem by giving the first deterministic algorithm that achieves the minimax-optimal Õ(ε⁻³) sample complexity for ε-multicalibration, a fairness property where predictions stay unbiased across reweighted subgroups. The authors generalize it to deterministic predictors satisfying outcome indistinguishability, yielding optimal deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors and answering questions left open by several prior works.

title: Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning
blurb: This paper introduces a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference, representing prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets so a transformer can adapt across families of priors at test time. Across evaluations including out-of-meta-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures, the method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while running orders of magnitude faster, with results validated on a spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark.

title: Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks
blurb: This paper introduces Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, problem constraints, and prior search experience into differentiable symbolic programs refined by evolutionary search and gradient-based parameter fitting. Across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS recovered known analytical forms and produced new interpretable ones, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics.

title: Low-Cost Multi-Precision Systolic Arrays for Accelerating FHE NTTs on AI ASICs
blurb: A multi-precision systolic array reconstructs full-precision FHE NTT results natively in-array, giving at least 1.33x speedup on AI matrix engines.

title: Agentic Electronic Design Automation: A Handoff Perspective
blurb: This survey reviews 82 LLM-agent systems for electronic design automation, organizing them around "handoff validity," the principle that a transferred design artifact must satisfy its downstream consumer's acceptance conditions and carry sufficient context, evidence, and provenance. The authors classify systems as Stage-Bound, Flow-Bound, or Organization-Bound, and propose a five-layer EDA agent communication protocol covering discovery, messaging, tool invocation, orchestration, and IP protection.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
blurb: This paper introduces the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a metric that measures how well a predictive model respects predefined logical constraints, independently of its predictive accuracy. RVS handles hard and soft rules differently, works on any model over a relational vocabulary, and compiles to auto-generated SQL queries for Horn rules, revealing that models with comparable accuracy can differ substantially in logical compliance.

title: Building TREX: Code execution and artifact generation for AI code review
blurb: Greptile built TREX (Test, Run, Execute), an execution layer for AI code review that runs the code under review instead of only reading the diff. The system catches runtime bugs that static analysis misses, like logic errors requiring specific state, post-load UI regressions, and race conditions, and shares the main Greptile reviewer's context rather than running as a separate agent to avoid duplicated exploration and wasted compute.

title: Introducing the MDN MCP server
blurb: Mozilla released the MDN MCP server, which brings MDN's web platform documentation and browser compatibility data directly into MCP-compatible coding agents and IDEs. The experimental server works with VS Code, Zed, Cursor, Claude Code, and Claude Desktop, giving agents access to current baseline support data rather than relying on potentially outdated training knowledge.

title: The data black hole at the center of AI
blurb: The author argues that AI progress has come not from improving sample efficiency but from dramatically widening the data distribution. RL is the main driver, functioning as synthetic data generation that burns compute against a verifier to find correct rollouts, but it still depends on bespoke human expert trajectories for every skill. That dependency is why the data-labeling industry now earns billions a year and is heading toward deca-billions.

title: Only half of US datacenter capacity planned for 2026 is actually  under construction
blurb: A report found that only about half of the US datacenter capacity slated to come online in 2026 is currently under construction, suggesting a wide gap between announced buildout plans and physical reality. The shortfall raises questions about whether projected AI compute supply will materialize on schedule, even as demand forecasts and capital commitments continue to climb.

title: Anthropic, Amazon, and the Fable shutdown; AI-powered school arrives; World Cup tech
blurb: The latest GeekWire Podcast covers Anthropic pulling its most powerful models offline after a U.S. order, with Amazon CEO Andy Jassy reportedly among the voices that raised the concerns behind it. The episode also examines how agentic AI is disrupting Amazon's "working backwards" process and visits Alpha School, a Seattle-area school that teaches core academics through AI-driven software rather than chatbots.

title: Zeta Labs brings AI employee Viktor to Microsoft Teams
blurb: Zeta Labs brought Viktor, its AI employee, to Microsoft Teams, extending a product already running in over 20,000 Slack workspaces.

title: AI Warfare Is at the Point of No Return. What Now?
blurb: A WSJ piece argues the US is stumbling into an AI arms race that is now too far along to reverse, citing a new executive order, a clash with Anthropic, and high-tech wars in Ukraine and beyond. The author contends global efforts to control military AI are falling behind the pace of deployment.

title: How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech
blurb: This paper adapts the DAAM cross-attention attribution framework to speech diffusion models for the first time, applying it to CapSpeech-TTS to trace how individual words in a style caption shape acoustic output. Analyzing 3,600 caption-transcript combinations, the authors find that style tokens act as global conditioning, correlate with pitch and energy, and peak in early ODE steps and deep layers, with attention selectivity maximized at layer 17.

title: Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy
blurb: This paper introduces "privacy via predictability," a fine-grained framework that measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information after observing an algorithm's output, given a known compromised portion of the dataset and a specified query family. The authors show predictability and differential privacy are generally incomparable, derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM using the generalized method of moments, and position the approach as complementary to DP.

title: Topological Data Analysis for High-Dimensional Dynamic Process Monitoring
blurb: This paper combines topological data analysis with a neural ODE to detect events in high-dimensional industrial process data, outperforming PCA, autoencoder, and Koopman baselines.

title: Library-Aware Doubles and Iterative Repair for Large Language Model-Generated Unit Tests in OpenSIL Firmware
blurb: This paper introduces an LLM-guided multi-agent pipeline that automates unit test authoring for AMD's open-source silicon initialization library (openSIL), where strict build constraints and unresolved dependencies routinely break compilation. The workflow combines test scaffold generation, library-aware stubs, mocks, and fakes, and an iterative compile-and-repair loop, producing compilable tests for 73 of 76 functions and reaching 98.8% mean line coverage with coverage guidance.

title: Encoding Your Domain Expert: The Context Layer Behind Spotify's Data Assistant
blurb: Spotify built a data assistant that translates plain-English questions into SQL across 70,000+ datasets, using a human-owned context layer that captures domain meaning schemas alone can't convey. Live since August 2025, it has served over 2,100 employees across 13,000+ conversations through a Slack bot, an MCP server, and a web UI, with more than a quarter of users having never written SQL.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the company behind the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B. The deal would fold one of the most widely used AI coding tools into Elon Musk's space and satellite empire, signaling SpaceX's push to build out internal AI software capabilities and developer infrastructure.

title: Ramp launches private SWE-Bench for financial coding agents
blurb: Ramp released a private SWE-Bench benchmark that evaluates coding agents on real-world tasks drawn from its own financial software codebase. The benchmark targets the gap between public coding benchmarks and production fintech work, measuring how agents handle domain-specific constraints, internal APIs, and the correctness requirements that financial systems demand.

title: Robinhood launches agentic trading via MCP server for all customers
blurb: Robinhood launched agentic trading through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, making it available to all customers. The integration lets AI agents connect directly to Robinhood accounts to execute trades and access portfolio data, extending agent-driven workflows into retail brokerage for the first time at this scale.

title: Why the Human Genome's Tangled Physicality May Confound AI
blurb: The author argues that the human genome resists the kind of understanding biologists once expected, since barely 2% of its 3 billion bases code for genes and the real difficulty lies in how those genes are regulated. Genomic foundation models like Evo 2, Genos, and DeepMind's AlphaGenome sidestep that complexity by learning correlations from known cases rather than mechanisms. The piece contends this will likely prove useful for prediction while leaving the deeper question of how the genome works inside a computational black box.

title: The Mythical Agent-Month
blurb: Wes McKinney revisits Fred Brooks's The Mythical Man-Month to ask whether its lessons hold when a single developer orchestrates a swarm of AI coding agents. He argues the human expert still feels essential, bringing rigor and continuous supervision through tools like roborev rather than "prompt and chill" vibe coding. The open question is whether parallel agents escape the old bottlenecks of scope creep, architectural drift, and coordination overhead, or simply reintroduce them at machine speed.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
blurb: Stripe and AWS introduced infrastructure that lets AI agents make payments on behalf of users while compensating the content owners and publishers whose material the agents access. The integration combines Stripe's payment rails with AWS Bedrock and AgentCore, giving publishers a way to meter and monetize agent traffic rather than have their content scraped for free.

title: Report: GKE Inference Gateway delivers up to 92% faster AI responses
blurb: Google's GKE Inference Gateway uses prefix caching and model-aware routing to direct LLM requests to accelerators already holding the relevant KV cache in memory, skipping recomputation of repeated prompt prefixes. An independent benchmark found it delivered 15.7% higher throughput, 92.8% shorter wait times, and 62.6% lower inter-token latency than the next leading managed Kubernetes service, with Snap reporting prefix cache hit rates of 75-80%.

title: Midjourney Medical
blurb: Midjourney, the bootstrapped AI image generator, launched a medical division built around a full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that images the body in 60 seconds using sound waves and water instead of radiation. The company plans to open its first Midjourney Spa in San Francisco in 2027 and scale to 50,000 scanners running a billion scans a month by 2031. The bet is that a generative-imaging company can carry its core competency from pixels into physical hardware and a regulated health market, funded off its own profits rather than venture money.

title: The Transfer Market
blurb: Wise has built a $19M-customer business serving not one audience but a patchwork of niches: expats paying foreign mortgages, workers supporting family abroad, small businesses moving money, and now travelling football fans buying World Cup dollars. Roughly 70% of growth comes through word-of-mouth, and the company is now spending upwards of 5% of revenue on marketing to scale beyond it. The hard part is branding one product that means something different to every fragment.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaves for OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, Google's VP of engineering and a co-lead on Gemini, is leaving for OpenAI less than two years after Google brought him back as part of its Character.AI deal. The move strips Google of one of its most senior model architects weeks after it shipped Gemini 3.5 Flash, and it lands as OpenAI confidentially files for an IPO. The talent war is now the main front in the model race, and the labs are paying whatever it takes to pull the handful of people who can actually push a frontier model forward.

title: SpaceX to acquire Cursor for $60B in stock, days after blockbuster IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B in all-stock, days after Cursor's blockbuster IPO, to shore up its struggling AI division. The pitch to IPO investors leaned on a claimed $26 trillion addressable market in AI, a number that has to absorb a frontier coding tool bolted onto a rocket company. The deal signals that the largest private hardware players now see AI tooling as core infrastructure worth paying public-market premiums to own outright, even when the strategic fit is far from obvious.

title: AI Startup Midjourney Pivots to Health With Ultrasound Machine
blurb: Midjourney, best known for AI image generation, announced its first hardware product and a move into personal health and medical devices, starting with an ultrasound machine shown at a San Francisco event. The pivot pushes a software-only company into regulated medical hardware, a market with FDA clearance timelines and manufacturing costs that have little in common with shipping a generative model. It is a reminder that the AI labs with strong brands and cash are willing to leave their original category entirely when they see a bigger opening.

title: Early Users of Anthropic Mythos Still Have Access After US Order
blurb: A subset of firms chosen by Anthropic to preview its Mythos model kept access even after a US government order forced the broader shutdown of every other version. Roughly 200 organizations, including banks, Cisco, and Dragos, are still running Mythos Preview through Project Glasswing, a cleared collective using the system to hunt for cyber vulnerabilities. The split shows how frontier access is increasingly governed by security carve-outs and government clearance, not just who is willing to pay for the model.

title: OpenAI is bringing on some big guns in the lead-up to its IPO
blurb: OpenAI is stacking its bench ahead of an IPO, hiring Transformer co-author Noam Shazeer from Google DeepMind and former White House AI policy official Dean Ball. Shazeer adds frontier research weight, while Ball will run a new Strategic Futures team shaping policy on catastrophic risk, recursive self-improvement, and lab-government relations. The pattern is the same one playing out across Google, Anthropic, and Meta: the labs are competing as hard for foundational researchers and policy operators as they are for compute, and OpenAI is loading up on both right before going public.

title: Waymo recalls about 3,900 robotaxis after some drove into 'freeway construction zones'
blurb: Waymo voluntarily recalled about 3,900 robotaxis to patch software after 13 incidents where its fifth-generation cars drove into active freeway construction zones in Phoenix and San Francisco. This is the company's second recall in just over a month, following a May recall over vehicles entering flooded zones, and it has restricted freeway operations while building a fix. The timing is awkward, as Waymo now runs in 11 U.S. markets, launched a $29.99 monthly subscription, and is planning its first international expansions to London and Tokyo this year.

title: Docs: OpenAI burned through $3.7B in Q1, on revenue of $5.7B, and ended the quarter with $73B+ in cash and marketable securities vs. $40B at the end of December (Erin Woo/The Information)
blurb: OpenAI burned through $3.7B in Q1 against $5.7B in revenue, yet its cash and marketable securities climbed to more than $73B from $40B at the end of December. The gap is fresh financing, not operating strength. The takeaway for founders is that frontier-scale AI now runs on a balance sheet topped up faster than it drains, and the burn keeps pace with revenue because compute capex scales with every new model and user.

title: AI inference startup Baseten reportedly raising $1.5B months after its last mega-round
blurb: Baseten is close to a $1.5B round at a $13B valuation, the WSJ reports, up 160% from the $5B it carried just five months ago. The fine print matters more than the headline: it is a split-priced round, with some investors coming in at $13B and others at $11B, a tactic that inflates the marquee number while making lead investors look good on paper. Baseten sits in the middle of the inference gold rush, routing requests to the cheapest competent model, and capital is chasing that layer faster than the businesses underneath it are maturing.

title: OpenAI opens ChatGPT Ads Manager beta to UK advertisers
blurb: OpenAI began rolling out its self-serve ChatGPT Ads Manager to UK advertisers, a beta with familiar campaign, billing, and targeting controls that signals the company is building real advertising infrastructure, not just experiments. If your startup sells on top of ChatGPT, the platform you depend on is now also monetizing the same attention you do. The unanswered questions are the ones that matter most: inventory, targeting depth, measurement, and how ads actually surface inside conversations, all of which will shape whether this becomes a channel worth budgeting for.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, days after the biggest IPO ever, sending its shares up 16% to make it the fourth most valuable US company. The deal folds Cursor into xAI to take on Anthropic and OpenAI in coding, but Cursor's market share has already slid from 41% to 26% in the past year while Anthropic now controls half the category. SpaceX is paying a premium for momentum that is fading, betting frontier AI distribution matters more than the share it bought.

title: Microsoft, Amazon Cloud Services Face Tough EU Antitrust Law
blurb: The EU is set to name AWS and Azure as gatekeepers under the Digital Markets Act, with the European Commission expected to share preliminary findings from its cloud probe as early as next week. That designation brings hard obligations on interoperability, data portability, and self-preferencing, aimed squarely at the lock-in that keeps enterprise workloads stuck on one provider. For founders building on top of the hyperscalers, the practical upside is easier multi-cloud and lower switching costs, but the near-term reality is years of legal fights before any of those rules actually bite.

title: Generative Engine Optimization at Scale: Measuring Brand Visibility Across AI Search Engines
blurb: Brand visibility on AI search engines now sorts into a clear three-tier ladder, with household names like Stripe appearing in 73% of relevant answers on the first run, mid-market brands in 44%, and niche players in just 11%. The highest-leverage content is the ranked "best-of" listicle, the single most-cited format at around 21% of all citations. For everyone outside the already-authoritative top brands, getting onto those lists is now the GEO move that actually moves the number.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer sees Anthropic as a national security threat and may ease the June 12 Commerce directive forcing the company to seek US approval before foreign nationals can touch its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The reversal came days after a G7 meeting with Dario Amodei, capping a three-month standoff that started when the Pentagon flagged Anthropic as a supply-chain risk over its refusal to strip military guardrails. The thaw lands right as Anthropic sits on a confidential IPO filing, where regulatory overhang is exactly the kind of risk that scares public-market buyers.

title: Companies rein in AI usage as costs strain budgets
blurb: Companies that rushed AI into everything are now pulling it back as the bills come due, with usage-based pricing turning every query into a line item that finance can't ignore. The pattern is the same across teams: frontier models wired into workflows that don't need them, costs scaling with adoption instead of value. The strain is forcing a reckoning on where AI actually earns its spend, and the budgets are tightening faster than the deployments can prove themselves.

title: The $13 Billion AI Startup Betting on Cheaper Alternatives to OpenAI, Anthropic
blurb: Baseten is raising $1.5B at a $13B valuation, betting that founders will pay for infrastructure to run cheaper open-source models instead of routing everything through OpenAI and Anthropic. It sits inside a growing Silicon Valley ecosystem built to serve low-cost AI, a market that only exists because open-source models are now close enough to frontier quality for most workloads. The bet is that the value migrates from the model layer to whoever makes it cheap and reliable to run.

title: The enormous size of the oil and gas market drives adoption of synthetic fuel production
blurb: New technology only scales when two things line up: a beachhead market willing to pay a premium while the tech is immature, and a learning rate that drops cost at least as fast as that beachhead gets exhausted. Tesla ran the playbook from Roadster to Model 3, and synthetic fuel follows the same path, except the target is the enormous oil and gas market that makes early production profitable far sooner. Wright's Law, not one giant plant, is what actually pushes the cost down.

title: TerraPower in deal with Meta for eight Natrium 345 MW nuclear plants
blurb: Meta agreed to fund up to eight of TerraPower's 345 MW Natrium reactors, its largest bet on advanced nuclear and its first direct investment in a new build, with the first units due as early as 2032. Each twin-reactor site delivers 690 MW of round-the-clock baseload, and at a hypothetical $6,000/kW the full eight-unit program runs near $17B. The deal signals that hyperscalers will increasingly underwrite their own power, locking up firm generation for AI data centers years before the reactors come online.

title: The May Core Update Made Reddit Google’s Default Answer
blurb: After May's core update, Reddit holds 1 in 10 of Google's top-3 results, and B2B categories like Career (17%) no longer get a pass.

title: Apple announces major App Store changes for Brazil, including alternative app marketplaces
blurb: Apple opened iOS in Brazil to alternative app marketplaces today, letting developers distribute outside the App Store and use non-Apple payment systems under a new commission structure modeled on Japan's. The settlement with competition watchdog CADE resolves a 2022 complaint from MercadoLibre, but the opening is narrow: no direct web sideloading, baseline Notarization review still applies, and age ratings plus parental controls follow apps everywhere. Brazil now joins the EU and Japan as a market where regulators, not Apple, set the distribution rules, and the patchwork of country-by-country App Store regimes keeps growing.

title: From $5B to $13B in five months: Baseten reportedly closing in on $1.5B raise
blurb: Baseten is reportedly closing in on a $1.5B round at up to a $13B valuation, less than three times its $5B mark from five months ago and roughly six times its valuation a year ago. The capital is chasing inference, not models, with the company citing estimates that inference will hit two-thirds of all AI compute by the end of 2026, up from a third in 2023. The deal is split-priced, some investors entering at an $11B floor and others paying the full $13B headline, a structure late-stage AI companies are using to raise large sums fast.

title: Hyundai buys Boston Dynamics
blurb: Hyundai is buying SoftBank's remaining 9.65% stake in Boston Dynamics for $325 million, giving the automaker full ownership of one of the few humanoid robotics companies with real factory work in sight. The strategic edge is that Hyundai doesn't have to imagine its first customer: it owns the plants, the vehicle programs, and now the robots, with a production Atlas slated to start parts sequencing at its Georgia EV plant by 2028. The bar is steep, with CEO Robert Playter saying Atlas needs to learn new tasks in a day or two and hit 99.9% reliability before it earns a place on the floor.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: Ahrefs checked server logs across 137K domains and found that 97% of published llms.txt files got zero traffic in May 2026, and 96% of the requests that did land came from bots, not AI assistants. The named AI tools that bother fetching them are coding agents like GPTBot and Claude-Code parsing developer docs, while AI search engines ignore the file entirely and never go looking for ones that don't exist. Unless you sell to developers reading your documentation, the file is buying you nothing, and your time is better spent on content the crawlers actually read.

title: Trump Tells Axios He Doesn’t See Anthropic as US Security Threat
blurb: Trump told Axios he doesn't view Anthropic as a national-security threat, days after his administration moved to cut off foreign access to the company's most advanced models. The two signals point in opposite directions, and that gap is the story for anyone building on frontier APIs. Export controls on model access are becoming a live policy lever, which means a US lab's geographic reach is now set in Washington as much as in its own product roadmap.

title: What Link data tells us about AI spending
blurb: Stripe analyzed spending across the 250 million customers paying with Link and found AI spend is accelerating sharply. The top 10% of AI spenders nearly doubled their monthly outlay from $183 in December 2025 to $359 in March 2026, a jump that took just three months after the prior climb from $84 to $183 took 22. The clearest signal is where the money goes: that same cohort now spends 5x more on app-builder platforms like Replit, Lovable, and Bolt than it did in January 2025.

title: The founder's playbook: Building an AI-native startup
blurb: Anthropic released a founder's playbook that remaps the four core startup stages — Idea, MVP, Launch, and Scale — for what AI makes possible in 2026, with goals, exit criteria, failure modes, and AI-powered exercises for each. The throughline is that the founder's job shifts from individual contributor to orchestrator, with non-technical founders shipping production apps and hitting revenue before scaling headcount. It is written for founders deciding how to architect the company around AI from day one, plus the early operators helping them get there.

title: Every fusion startup that has raised over $100M
blurb: Private fusion has crossed from punchline to fundable, with multiple startups now past $100M raised on the back of better chips, AI-driven reactor simulations, and high-temperature superconducting magnets. Commonwealth Fusion Systems alone holds about a third of all private fusion capital, closing an $863M round in August to bring its total near $3B as it builds Sparc in Massachusetts. The science cleared scientific breakeven at a U.S. lab in 2022, but commercial breakeven, where a plant outputs more than the whole facility draws, is still the bet investors are funding.

title: The 11 standout startups from YC’s Demo Day, according to VCs
blurb: YC's Spring 2026 cohort pushed Demo Day valuations to new highs, with at least two startups fetching $175M or more and investors paying a clear premium for proven, repeat founders. The buzziest names skewed toward defense tech, robotics, AI infrastructure, and agents, led by counter-drone startup 9 Mothers, valued at upward of $200M after booking $1.6M in sales and a contract pipeline it pitches reaching $1B. The signal for founders is that capital is concentrating early around hard-tech and infrastructure bets with visible demand, not consumer apps.

title: Paris-based Kyber, which develops a low-latency remote device control SDK and is founded by VLC lead developer Jean-Baptiste Kempf, raised $5M led by Lightspeed (Anna Heim/TechCrunch)
blurb: Kyber, a Paris-based startup building a low-latency SDK for remote device control, raised $5M led by Lightspeed. Founder Jean-Baptiste Kempf is the lead developer behind VLC, the open-source media player installed billions of times, and that track record in squeezing latency out of streaming is the core bet here. The round signals investor appetite for infrastructure that makes controlling hardware over a network feel local, a primitive that cloud gaming, robotics, and remote workstations all depend on.

title: Lithuanian startup launches open-source network to detect Shahed-type drones
blurb: Lithuanian startup Mainline launched Drone Radar, an open-source network that turns volunteers' spare Android phones into acoustic sensors that listen for the low-frequency engine signatures of Shahed-type drones and triangulate their location when several nearby devices catch the same signal. The team of 20 is aiming for 10,000 active participants across the Baltics and Poland, with plans to add audio from home surveillance cameras and sensors on mobile operators' towers. It points to a wider pattern of crowdsourced, low-cost defense infrastructure built from consumer hardware rather than procurement contracts.

title: Agentic marketing AI startup Gradial grabs $65M in fresh funding
blurb: Gradial raised $65M in a Series C led by Insight Partners, valuing the Seattle marketing-AI startup at $675M and bringing its 16-month total to $110M. While most software companies bolt agents onto their existing products, Gradial is betting on the gaps between tools, running a single layer of agents across Salesforce, ServiceNow, Databricks, and Adobe to author, brand-check, and publish marketing content through a company's existing approval flows. The bet is on orchestration over point tools, a wave of startups now competing to be the connective layer for enterprise agentic deployments.

title: Snowflake’s CMO Runs Marketing for 700 People. She Starts Her Day By Talking to Her Data, Not a Dashboard.
blurb: Snowflake's CMO no longer opens a dashboard in the morning. She queries her data in plain English, getting the "why" behind pipeline movement in real time instead of pinging her team and scheduling alignment meetings to argue over whose numbers are right. The lesson underneath the demo is that AI amplifies whatever data you feed it, so the operators who win invest in their data estate first, and the 2026 mandate is now 40-50% growth with flat headcount because leadership expects AI, not new hires, to absorb the load.

title: How Attention.com Turns Sales Calls Into Pipeline: The Best GTM Data You Own, and Why Most B2B Teams Throw It Away
blurb: Your next quarter's pipeline is already sitting in the sales conversations you had this quarter, and most B2B teams never mine it. Attention.com's playbook runs an agent monthly over recent closed-won deals, extracts buyer function, tenure, company size, and firmographics, then feeds those personas into an enrichment tool to build a dozen specific buyer pockets instead of one broad ICP. The shift that matters is cadence: an annual ICP exercise is a 2021 habit, and the teams compounding fastest refresh it off live closed-won data every month or quarter.

title: Google DeepMind Explores the Path to ASI
blurb: Google DeepMind released a report mapping how AI might keep advancing past human-level AGI toward artificial superintelligence, a system more capable than entire organizations of people. It lays out four routes to get there — scaling AGI, paradigm shifts, recursive self-improvement, and intelligence emerging from large multi-agent collectives — and the frictions that could stall each one. The takeaway for founders is that AGI may not be a single step change to plan around, but a long run of compounding breakthroughs across science and technology, with the timing deeply uncertain.

title: Source: Elastic agrees to buy CRV-backed DeductiveAI for up to $85M
blurb: Elastic agreed to buy CRV-backed DeductiveAI for up to $85M, a fast exit for a startup founded in 2023 that raised a $7.5M seed at a $33M valuation and reached only about $1M in ARR. The deal sits in AI site reliability engineering, a sector growing because AI-written code is flooding systems faster than human SREs can debug them. It also fits the pattern where incumbents buy AI-native startups to bolt agentic capability onto existing suites, with Elastic folding Deductive's auto-remediation into its observability platform rather than building it in-house.

title: Sources: Bain Capital stands to make $15B+ in profits on its 2018 Kioxia buyout, a ~20x return, as Kioxia's stock has surged 5,000%+ since its December 2024 IPO (Financial Times)
blurb: Bain Capital stands to clear more than $15B on its 2018 Kioxia buyout, roughly a 20x return, after the chipmaker's stock surged over 5,000% since its December 2024 IPO. The win rests on a single contrarian bet: buying memory at the bottom of a brutal NAND downcycle and holding through it. The lesson for anyone deploying capital is that the largest returns still come from cyclical, capital-heavy businesses bought when sentiment is worst, not from the asset-light software the market prefers.

title: Sources: Abu Dhabi's MGX is exploring buying Singapore-based data center operator DayOne; last month, sources said DayOne planned a US IPO at a $20B valuation (Reuters)
blurb: Abu Dhabi's MGX is exploring a buyout of DayOne, the Singapore-based data center operator that just last month was reportedly lining up a US IPO at a $20B valuation. The pivot from public listing to sovereign-wealth acquisition shows where the real money for AI infrastructure now sits, with Gulf state funds increasingly willing to take whole data center platforms private rather than let them tap public markets. For founders building anything compute-adjacent, the buyers with the deepest pockets are no longer Silicon Valley incumbents but state-backed capital chasing the physical layer of the AI boom.

title: SpaceX IPO Sparks Anticipation for OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: SpaceX closed its first week of public trading up 37% from its IPO price, and investors are now reading that demand as a signal for OpenAI and Anthropic, both expected to go public soon. The strong debut suggests public markets are ready to absorb the largest private tech names at the valuations they have commanded in late-stage rounds. For founders, it sets the comparison every AI-adjacent IPO will be measured against, and it pulls forward the exit timeline that has kept venture's paper wealth locked up.

title: Accenture Takes a Hit on Worsening Outlook and Cloudy AI Future
blurb: Accenture shares fell 18% after the consulting giant said it will book less revenue than expected in the coming months, with management pointing to a softer outlook and uncertainty about how AI reshapes IT services. The selloff is a warning for any business whose model rests on selling human implementation hours. As clients spend on AI tools that compress the work, the firms that resold large teams of consultants are the ones most exposed to the squeeze.

title: Prem seeks $100M Series A as export bans boost sovereign AI demand
blurb: Swiss startup Prem is raising $100M at a valuation of at least $500M to sell software that runs and fine-tunes AI models entirely on a customer's own infrastructure, with data that never leaves. The pitch landed at the right moment: after the US ordered Anthropic to block foreign access to its newest models on June 12, and JPMorgan and Goldman cut Claude access in Greater China, a frontier model stopped being a tool for regulated firms and became a dependency someone else controls. Gartner sees roughly $80B in sovereign cloud spend this year, with European budgets projected to more than triple by 2027.

title: Game-clip AI startup General Intuition in talks to raise $300M at $2B valuation
blurb: General Intuition is in talks to raise about $300M at a valuation just over $2B, four times the $134M seed it took eight months ago. The startup trains embodied AI agents on roughly 2 billion first-person game clips a year from Medal's 10M-plus monthly users, betting that a player's own view teaches a machine to read and act in physical space better than spectator footage. The data is the moat here, not the model. OpenAI reportedly offered $500M for Medal in 2024 and was turned down, and the world-model field has already pulled in billions this year.

title: 6 Key Signs a VP of Sales Can’t Scale Beyond $5m-$10m ARR
blurb: The VPs who get you to $5M ARR often can't get you past $10M, and the warning signs show up early. The ones who stall stay only loosely organized after $5M, hire weak or lookalike managers under them, and make excuses as the quarterly number scales from a record month to a record week. The clearest tell is when a VP threatens to quit at the mention of a CRO above them, because the leaders who actually scale know that hire is coming and say the same thing to their own reports.

title: NVIDIA joins $54M bet on startup that gets AI data centres online three years faster
blurb: Verse raised $54M in a Series B led by Bessemer, with NVIDIA also writing a check, to shrink the grid bottleneck choking AI buildouts. The startup uses on-site battery storage to skip interconnection queues that now run five to seven years at major hubs, getting data centers online up to three years sooner without cutting compute. The real signal is NVIDIA folding Verse into its DSX AI Factory reference architecture, putting the software in front of every operator building to NVIDIA's spec as the race for compute becomes a race for power.

title: Startup Backed by Ex-Google CEO Debuts Robot, LG Partnership
blurb: A startup backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt debuted an AI-powered industrial robot built in partnership with LG, as capital keeps flooding into humanoids. The robotics push is no longer about consumer demos but about putting machines on factory floors, where labor shortages and reshoring give the economics a clearer payback. Schmidt's name and an LG manufacturing partner signal where the smart money thinks the near-term value sits: industrial automation that ships, not household helpers that impress in a video.

title: How to Pivot Well
blurb: Knowing when to pivot, how fast, and toward what has quietly become a core founder skill in the AI era, since shifting model capabilities make a new pivot theoretically possible every quarter. A good pivot keeps something planted, usually the team and the investor's cash, while everything else moves, and the worst failure often comes from refusing to pivot at all once it's clear the current bet is wrong. Speedrun's investors argue inexperienced founders pivot best because they have the least to unlearn and aren't boxed into one market.

title: Why we're bullish on loops
blurb: PostHog argues you should stop prompting agents and instead build self-prompting loops with a goal, context, evaluation, and an agent.

title: Noam Shazeer is joining OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-author of the original Transformer paper and one of the most influential researchers in modern AI, is leaving Google to join OpenAI. He called it a difficult decision after years building with the team at Google, where his work underpinned much of the field's current architecture. The move signals that the talent war at the frontier is still being fought one senior researcher at a time, and the labs that win it are the ones willing to pry loose the people who defined the last decade of progress.

title: Indian Software Stocks Tumble After Accenture Warns on Growth
blurb: Indian software stocks slid Friday after Accenture forecast slower revenue growth, joining a global tech selloff in a sector that has already lost nearly 30% of its market value this year. TCS fell as much as 6.5% to its lowest since 2020, while Infosys dropped 8.7%, tracking a 10% overnight slide in its US-listed shares. The warning signals that AI is now eating into the IT services outsourcing model the Indian giants were built on, and the market is repricing that risk.

title: An interview with Smartbird CEO Nadia Carlsten about the shoe company Allbirds becoming an AI infrastructure company, plans to deploy compute clusters, and more (Tim Fernholz/TechCrunch)
blurb: Allbirds, the sustainable shoe company, is pivoting into AI infrastructure under the Smartbird banner, with CEO Nadia Carlsten laying out plans to deploy compute clusters. It is the latest sign that the AI buildout is pulling capital and reinvention from corners of the market that have nothing to do with software, as a consumer brand bets its future on selling compute instead of footwear. The move tracks a broader pattern of struggling public companies repositioning around AI to reset their story and their valuation.

title: An Interview with Michael Morton About E-Commerce in the Age of AI
blurb: Michael Morton argues that the bear cases against AI in e-commerce are unfalsifiable, which is exactly what makes them dangerous to plan around. The sharper question is distribution versus referral: whether AI agents become a new channel you optimize for or a layer that disintermediates the merchant relationship entirely. The interview extends the same logic to grocery and autonomous vehicles, where the hard part is operations, not the model.

title: An interview with Roblox CEO Dave Baszucki on his early decision not to prioritize ad revenue, whether every mega platform becomes an everything app, and more (Tyler Cowen/Conversations with Tyler)
blurb: Roblox CEO Dave Baszucki tells Tyler Cowen that he deliberately passed on advertising revenue early on, betting that a healthier creator economy would compound into something larger than ad dollars ever could. He also pushes back on the idea that every mega platform inevitably collapses into an everything app, arguing the pull toward one is weaker than it looks. The throughline is a founder choosing long-term platform incentives over the obvious near-term monetization lever.

title: The data black hole at the center of AI
blurb: Modern AI hasn't gotten more sample-efficient. It has gotten better because labs widened the data distribution, pouring compute into RL to generate synthetic rollouts and buying mountains of bespoke human expert trajectories for every skill they want a model to master. That dependency is why the data-labeling industry behind these expert labels and RL environments already earns billions a year and is heading toward tens of billions.

title: Artisan’s Ava 2.0: What a Fully Autonomous AI BDR Actually Looks Like in Production with CEO Jaspar Carmichael-Jack
blurb: Artisan's pitch for Ava, its autonomous AI BDR, is accountability rather than automation: instead of a taped-together stack of sequencer, enrichment, and copywriting tools where no single piece owns the outcome, the UI rolls every campaign up to one number, the credits you paid per positive reply or booked meeting. The proof points back it up, with SaaStr's own outbound hitting a 3.6% positive response rate across 7,000 emails on Ava 1.0 and a YC-founder campaign coached to read human clearing 4%. The lesson for operators is that buying a result beats buying software you have to assemble yourself.

title: Australia is the world’s fastest-growing venture ecosystem of the past decade
blurb: Australia is now the world's fastest-growing venture ecosystem, growing 13.7 times faster than any other major hub over the past decade and producing more $10B companies per dollar invested than anywhere else. The catch is that early-stage investment has fallen every year since 2021, and founders now raise 41% of their early-stage capital from overseas, double the share in Europe or the US. The headline returns are real, with Australian VC delivering a 24.4% pooled five-year return, but the domestic capital that should be funding the next Canva is barely reaching seed.

title: AI buildout gives tech investors new reasons to watch bond market
blurb: Tech's balance-sheet giants used to shrug off rate moves, but the AI buildout has flipped that. Amazon, Alphabet, Microsoft, and Meta are projected to deploy a combined $750 billion this year, up more than 80% from 2025, and a big piece of it is funded by debt as Nvidia, Oracle, and others tap bond markets for tens of billions each. That puts megacap tech back at the mercy of the 10-year yield, now near 4.45%, and means founders watching these names now have to watch the Fed too.

title: Jio Platforms files for India’s largest-ever IPO, with nearly $3 billion earmarked for debt repayment
blurb: Jio Platforms filed for a $3.8B IPO that would be India's largest ever, surpassing Hyundai Motor India's $3.3B record, at a valuation north of $130B. Nearly $2.9B of the proceeds is earmarked to prepay dollar and yen borrowings held by its telecom unit, wiping out most of the foreign currency debt that stood at 275.8 billion rupees in March, down from 484.4 billion two years earlier. The entire raise is a fresh issue with no offer-for-sale, so every rupee lands on the balance sheet rather than cashing out existing holders.

title: Nuvei to buy Payoneer for $2.75B
blurb: Nuvei agreed to buy cross-border payments firm Payoneer for $2.75B, folding Payoneer's SMB and freelancer network into a payments stack that Advent took private last year. The deal hands Nuvei a base of millions of small businesses and marketplace sellers moving money across borders, the exact customers most payment platforms struggle to acquire one at a time. For founders running global or marketplace businesses, it is another sign that cross-border payouts are consolidating into a few large players, which usually means fewer independent vendors and more bundled pricing to negotiate against.

title: $10,000,000 on the line: how we measure Devin's engineering output
blurb: Cognition is putting up to $10M per customer behind a single claim: Devin ships more engineering output than the customer pays for. To back it, the team built a measurement system that quantifies engineering output and validated it against independent data rather than its own benchmarks. The move ties pricing directly to delivered value, the same outcome-based shift spreading across AI vendors, and it raises the bar for any competitor still selling seats instead of results.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
