blurb: Stripe and AWS launched infrastructure that lets AI agents pay content owners and publishers directly, so an agent that reads or uses a publisher's content can settle the transaction automatically. Stripe handles the payment rails and AWS supplies the agent runtime, giving publishers a way to charge machines the way they once charged subscribers. For founders building agents, it means programmatic access to paywalled content is becoming a billable, metered transaction rather than a scraping fight.

title: Apple Foundation Models
blurb: Anthropic released Claude for Foundation Models, a Swift package that conforms Claude to the `LanguageModel` protocol in Apple's Foundation Models framework, so apps drive it through the same `LanguageModelSession` API used for Apple's on-device model. Requests go straight to the Claude API rather than through Apple, and developers swap between on-device and server-side models per session to add larger context, frontier reasoning, or server tools like web search and code execution.

title: Beyond LoRA: Can you beat the most popular fine-tuning technique?
blurb: This Hugging Face post argues that engineers default to LoRA for parameter-efficient fine-tuning without considering the dozens of alternatives, and walks through the PEFT library's unified API for testing methods like quantized adapters and other low-memory approaches. It covers when PEFT is worth it over prompting or full fine-tuning, plus LoRA's underdiscussed perks: tiny checkpoints, resistance to catastrophic forgetting, and serving many fine-tunes from one base model.

title: TypeScript 7.0 RC
blurb: Microsoft released the TypeScript 7.0 release candidate, a port of the compiler from JavaScript to Go that runs roughly 10x faster than 6.0 thanks to native code and shared-memory parallelism. The type-checking logic was methodically ported rather than rewritten, so semantics match 6.0 exactly, and teams at Figma, Google, Slack, and Vercel have already run it on multi-million-line codebases. Install it with `npm install -D typescript@rc`.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Simon Willison released datasette-apps, a plugin that hosts self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside a sandboxed iframe on your Datasette instance, where they can run read-only SQL queries against your data and write queries through configured stored queries. The sandbox uses `allow-scripts allow-forms` plus an injected CSP header to block cookie and localStorage access and outbound HTTP requests, preventing a buggy or malicious app from exfiltrating private data.

title: UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents
blurb: UltraQuant compresses the KV cache to 4 bits for long-context, multi-turn agents, combining Walsh-Hadamard rotation, asymmetric K/V treatment, and an FP4 path with FP8 queries, UE8M0 group scales, and scaled-MFMA kernels on AMD CDNA4 GPUs. On a cache-pressured agentic workload it cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in late rounds and lifts output throughput 1.63x over the vLLM FP8 KV baseline.

title: N-Version Programming with Coding Agents
blurb: This paper applies N-version programming to AI coding agents, generating 48 implementations of the Knight-Leveson Launch Interceptor spec across different agents, models, and languages, then testing them on a shared oracle with 1,000,000 randomized inputs. Majority voting across three-version units dropped mean failures from 387.44 to 130.99, and 11,844 N-version units showed zero observed failures, though common-mode failures persisted where the specification was ambiguous.

title: 7,000 Langflow servers are under attack. LangGraph and LangChain have the same holes
blurb: Three of the most widely deployed AI agent frameworks — LangGraph, Langflow, and LangChain — were each found to turn an ordinary bug class into remote code execution or secret theft, with roughly 7,000 Langflow servers already under active in-the-wild attack. Researchers chained a SQL injection in LangGraph's SQLite checkpointer to RCE, traced a path traversal in Langflow's file upload endpoint to live exploitation, and documented a LangChain-core prompt loader flaw that reads credentials straight off disk.

title: The Correctness Illusion in LLM-Generated GPU Kernels
blurb: This paper shows that standard GPU kernel benchmarks like KernelBench, TritonBench, and GEAK certify buggy LLM-generated kernels as correct because they check only fixed shapes, dtypes, and tolerances with allclose. Re-running 24 Triton and CPU kernels under schema-aware seeded fuzzing against an fp64 reference caught all 9 seeded bugs and passed all 15 controls, with identical verdicts across five GPU classes.

title: There are no instances in ATProto
blurb: Dan Abramov argues that "instances" are a category error in atproto, which separates hosting from aggregation the way blogs separated publishing from readers like Google Reader, rather than enclosing users inside self-hosted communities the way Mastodon does. Your data lives in a personal repository you control, and apps are mere projections over the network, so there is no server you "join" and no community walls to pick between.

title: Is it agentic enough? Benchmarking open models on your own tooling
blurb: Hugging Face introduces a benchmark that measures not just whether a coding agent solves a task with a library like transformers, but how much work it takes, fanning model × revision × task runs across Hugging Face Jobs on identical hardware. The post argues libraries now need agent-friendly design, a clear CLI, extensive structured docs, and self-contained examples, since a clunky API or stale docs sends the agent down a longer, more expensive path.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 across all supported Pixel devices, with the first official release shipping tomorrow.

title: Project Valhalla, Explained: How a Decade of Work Arrives in JDK 28
blurb: On June 15, Oracle engineer Lois Foltan confirmed that JEP 401: Value Classes and Objects, the core of Project Valhalla, is targeting JDK 28 in a pull request adding over 197,000 lines across 1,816 files. This deep-dive traces the decade-long effort to make user-defined types "code like a class, work like an int," and cautions that the JDK 28 cut is preview-only, disabled by default, and just the first part of the project.

title: Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents
blurb: Probe-and-refine tuning iteratively diagnoses and patches a repository's agent guidance file using synthetic bug-fix probes through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified, it lifts Qwen3.5-35B's resolve rate to 33.0% from 28.3% for a static knowledge base, with the gain coming from coverage rather than precision: better guidance helps agents reach the correct file while per-patch precision holds near 59%.

title: How we built an internal data analytics agent
blurb: GitHub built Qubot, an internal Copilot-powered agent that lets any employee query the company's data warehouse in plain language and get answers in seconds. The architecture splits into three parts — a Slack/VS Code/CLI interface, a context layer, and a query engine — with each Slack question spawning a Copilot Cloud Agent that returns a markdown report in a pull request for later reuse.

title: AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning
blurb: AutoPass is a training-free multi-agent framework that queries LLVM internals to tune compiler passes, beating -O3 by up to 1.117x.

title: Migrate from OpenClaw
blurb: The `hermes claw migrate` command imports an existing OpenClaw setup, including legacy Clawdbot and Moltbot directories, into Nous Research's Hermes agent, mapping config keys for personas, memory, skills, MCP servers, and TTS while archiving anything without a Hermes equivalent. The guide details a dry-run preview before any changes, four-source API key resolution under `--migrate-secrets`, and a `--portal` flag that collapses multi-provider setups into one OAuth login covering 300+ models.

title: npm v12’s Biggest Security Change: From Implicit to Explicit Trust
blurb: npm v12 (estimated July 2026) flips package installation from implicit to explicit trust, blocking three high-risk vectors by default: lifecycle scripts (allowScripts), Git-repository installs (--allow-git), and remote-URL installs (--allow-remote). JFrog's research team ties those mechanisms to roughly 53% of malicious npm attacks over the past year, shifting responsibility for installation-time execution from external scanners onto npm itself.

title: Anthropic launches live Artifacts for Claude Code
blurb: Anthropic launched Artifacts for Claude Code in beta, turning coding sessions into live, shareable web pages like PR walkthroughs, dashboards, and release checklists that update in real time as the session runs. Each artifact is built from full session context including codebase, connectors, and conversation history, with version history and org-scoped access controls, available now to Claude Team and Enterprise via the CLI and desktop app.

title: Is AI ruining our skills? Early results are in – and they're not good
blurb: Evidence is emerging that heavy AI reliance erodes the expertise of skilled professionals, with a study of Polish endoscopists finding their unassisted adenoma detection rate fell from 28.4% to 22.4% after they began using an AI diagnostic tool. The piece argues workers in medicine and software should deliberately choose which skills to keep sharp and which to outsource, since dependence can quietly degrade the abilities their jobs still require.

title: Surprising economics of load-balanced systems
blurb: Modeling a load-balanced fleet as an M/M/c queue, the author shows that mean request latency asymptotically approaches the one-second service time as server count grows, even with per-server load held constant at 80%. The counterintuitive result is that bigger fleets queue a smaller fraction of requests, so scaling out improves observed latency for free rather than just adding throughput, with the same trend holding at the tail percentiles.

title: Phoenix: Safe GitHub Issue Resolution via Multi-Agent LLMs
blurb: Phoenix is a multi-agent LLM system that resolves GitHub issues end to end, splitting the work across six specialized agents (planner, reproducer, coder, tester, failure analyst, PR) behind seven layered safety controls and a baseline-aware test check. On a 24-instance SWE-bench Lite slice it oracle-resolves 75% with no pass-to-pass regressions, though manual review found half the resulting PRs place code at incorrect paths.

title: GitLab 19.1 released
blurb: GitLab 19.1 shipped secret false positive detection with the GitLab Duo Agent Platform as generally available, automatically analyzing each critical and high severity secret finding after a scan and adding an AI assessment, confidence score, and reasoning to the vulnerability report. The release also adds an always-on availability mode that lets administrators force GitLab Duo on across an entire instance or top-level group, blocking project owners from disabling it for centralized AI governance.

title: Researchers grow a hypothesis tree for AI coding agents
blurb: Researchers from Renmin University and Microsoft Research introduced Arbor, a persistent hypothesis tree that lets AI coding agents retain experiments and lessons across long research sessions instead of losing them on context resets. A long-lived coordinator manages strategy while short-lived executors run isolated worktrees to test competing hypotheses, a design that delivered 2.5x better performance than standard agents on real engineering tasks under the same budget.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, with the deal expected to close in Q3 pending regulatory approval. The purchase folds Cursor into SpaceX's xAI unit to compete with Anthropic and OpenAI on coding tools, though Cursor's market share has slipped from 41% to roughly 26% over the past year as Anthropic captured half the category.

title: AI coding agents may be getting bad instructions from ‘smelly’ config files
blurb: Researchers at Brazil's Federal University of Minas Gerais published the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding-agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, identifying six recurring flaws including lint leakage, context bloat, skill leakage, and conflicting instructions. They found at least one smell in 91 of 100 popular open-source repos, with lint leakage in 62% of files and context bloat in 42%, all of which inflate token costs and degrade how agents interpret project conventions.

title: Beyond Static Endpoints: Tool Programs as an Interface for Flexible Agentic Web Services
blurb: ToolPro represents an agent's tool intent as an executable program that encodes multi-step service interactions with loops, conditionals, and retries, rather than forcing static endpoint calls. Running over MCP-style services in a WebAssembly sandbox, it uses effect-aware replay for exactly-once state-modifying calls and cuts end-to-end latency by up to 53.4% and client-side traffic by up to 96.1%, with bigger gains under higher network latency.

title: How software development’s speed obsession enabled TeamPCP’s chaos crusade
blurb: The threat actor TeamPCP has injected malicious code into more than 1,000 software packages in under four months, starting with Trivy in February and exploiting the open-source trust model that lets organizations ingest unvetted dependencies. The author argues the attacks are notable for scale rather than novelty, and that AI coding agents installing unvetted packages with no human in the loop have widened an already-known gap in CI/CD pipelines.

title: Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages
blurb: Multi-LCB extends LiveCodeBench beyond Python to twelve programming languages by transforming existing LCB tasks into equivalent problems while preserving the benchmark's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Evaluating 24 instruction and reasoning models, the authors found clear Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and wide gaps in multilingual code-generation performance.

title: Google sets timeline for Android developer verification enforcement
blurb: Google set September 30, 2026 as the enforcement date for Android developer verification, starting with users in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, where apps distributed through stores like Google Play, Galaxy Store, and GetApps must be linked to a verified identity before installation. New APIs let developers register package names in bulk or via CI/CD, and a fee-free limited distribution account lets hobbyists ship to up to 20 devices without a government ID.

title: Hey, n00b, we didn't hire you to complete tasks
blurb: Kent Beck argues that junior engineers are hired as an option premium on the engineer they will become, not for the tasks they complete, since a senior could clear that task backlog faster anyway. Seniors are sorting new hires into A, B, and C performers, so the signal that matters is not throughput but evidence of growth, judgment, and making the people around you more productive.

title: CSSQuake
blurb: A playable Quake-style FPS rendered entirely in CSS, with multiplayer, dynamic lighting, and particle effects.

title: AWS aims to take the pain out of RAG with Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base
blurb: AWS released Bedrock Managed Knowledge Base, a managed RAG service that auto-selects and maintains the embeddings, re-ranker, and foundation models behind enterprise retrieval, plus six native connectors for S3, SharePoint, Confluence, Google Drive, OneDrive, and web content. Smart Parsing and an Agentic Retriever target accuracy across mixed content types and multi-repository queries, with the trade-off being less control over model choice and pipeline internals.

title: Anthropic's Claude Code Artifacts update brings live, shared dashboards and interactive workspaces to enterprises
blurb: Anthropic added Artifacts to Claude Code on Team and Enterprise plans, turning a session's work into a live, shareable HTML page that builds from the local repo, connected data sources, and monitoring tools without standing up extra infrastructure. The page refreshes in-place at a fixed URL as the agent works and versions every change for rollback, landing about two weeks after OpenAI shipped a similar enterprise hosting feature called Sites for Codex.

title: New AI optimization framework beats Claude Code and Codex by 2.5x on the same compute budget
blurb: Researchers at Renmin University of China and Microsoft Research released Arbor, an autonomous optimization framework that organizes hypotheses, experiments, and insights into a tree so the agent learns from prior failures instead of repeating trial-and-error. On real-world engineering tasks under the same compute budget, Arbor delivered more than 2.5x the verifiable performance gains of standard coding agents like Claude Code and Codex.

title: Apple announces major App Store changes for Brazil, including alternative app marketplaces
blurb: Apple opened iOS in Brazil to alternative app marketplaces and outside payment methods for users on iOS 26.5 and up, settling a CADE dispute that started with a 2022 MercadoLibre complaint. Apps distributed off the App Store still go through baseline Notarization and must carry age ratings, and the changes bar direct web sideloading while introducing a Japan-style commission structure.

title: ICYMI: ZAI launches GLM-5.2 open model with 1M context
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an open-weight coding model with a 1M-token context window and up to 128K output tokens, built for long-horizon agent work across full repositories. The 744B-parameter model scores 81.0 on Terminal-Bench 2.1 and 62.1 on SWE-bench Pro, uses an IndexShare sparse-attention scheme to cut per-token FLOPs 2.9x at 1M context, and plugs into Claude Code, Cline, and OpenClaw via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

title: From SQLi to RCE - Exploiting LangGraph's Checkpointer
blurb: Check Point researcher Yarden Porat shows how an unsanitized filter key in LangGraph's SQLite checkpointer flows straight into a json_extract SQL clause, turning get_state_history() into SQL injection that escalates to remote code execution. The managed LangSmith Deployment runs PostgreSQL and is unaffected, but self-hosted users should upgrade to langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite 3.0.1+, langgraph 1.0.10+, or langgraph-checkpoint-redis 1.0.2+.

title: I hate compilers
blurb: The author needed reproducible builds of `wasm2js` for Anubis, but identical source produced byte-different binaries across machines, so they vendored a deterministic copy compiled to WebAssembly via wasi-sdk. Getting there meant passing `--no-wasm-opt` to stop Clang silently invoking a system tool during linking, and disabling address-space randomization with `setarch` to kill ~29-byte diffs from pointer-dependent catch-reference ordering, with separate x86_64 and arm64 checksums as the remaining trade-off.

title: Emacs 31 is around the corner: The changes I'm daily driving
blurb: Emacs 31 is in pre-release, and the author has been daily-driving builds from the emacs-31 and master branches for months, folding each new feature into a no-external-packages config. The standout change is built-in tree-sitter support: setting `treesit-enabled-modes` to `t` auto-switches major modes to their tree-sitter variants, while `treesit-auto-install-grammar` fetches and compiles missing grammars on demand, replacing the old manual `treesit-language-source-alist` ceremony.

title: SteamOS Linux 3.8 released as stable
blurb: Valve promoted SteamOS 3.8 to its stable channel, shipping the Arch-based handheld OS with an updated Linux kernel, a newer Mesa graphics stack, and broader hardware support beyond the Steam Deck. The release continues Valve's push to run SteamOS on third-party handhelds and desktops, giving Linux gaming developers a more current Proton and driver baseline to target.

title: AI Coding Agent Horror Stories: The 13-Hour AWS Outage
blurb: A Docker post-mortem walks through how an AI coding agent's unsupervised changes cascaded into a 13-hour AWS outage, tracing the failure from a plausible-looking automated fix to the production blast radius it triggered. The writeup uses the incident to argue for guardrails around agent-driven deploys, including human review gates, scoped permissions, and sandboxed execution before changes reach live infrastructure.

title: When failover isn't safe: Building high-availability PostgreSQL on Kubernetes
blurb: Datadog rearchitected its Kubernetes-based PostgreSQL clusters after a gameday zonal-failure test exposed that primaries kept accepting writes while replicas fell behind, leaving no replica current enough to promote safely. The fix uses synchronous replication for failover candidates coordinated by Patroni, trading some write latency for guaranteed durability so failover becomes both automatic and lossless.

title: Framer 3.0
blurb: Framer 3.0 adds Agents, which design entire pages on the canvas, create components, write code, connect to the CMS, and iterate with you across breakpoints and effects. The release also ships Branching, so larger teams can isolate changes and adopt the AI features without overwriting each other's work, alongside a new Community for sharing and selling templates.

title: The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud
blurb: This paper proposes a bi-channel networking paradigm for cloud databases, splitting traffic into a high-performance user-space UDP data path for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive operations and a kernel TCP control path for coordination and recovery. The co-design sidesteps the kernel TCP CPU overhead that bottlenecks fast cloud networks, saturating 200 Gbit/s in a distributed shuffle with three cores while preserving TCP's reliability guarantees.

title: .gitignore Isn't the only way to ignore files in Git
blurb: Git supports three levels of file ignoring beyond the standard checked-in .gitignore: `.git/info/exclude` for per-repo rules that stay out of version control, and `~/.config/git/ignore` for machine-wide patterns like .DS_Store. The post covers configuring a custom global ignore path via `core.excludesFile` and using `git check-ignore -v` to trace which file and line is excluding a given path.

title: Local Qwen isn't a worse Opus, it's a different tool
blurb: The author argues that local Qwen models aren't a budget Opus substitute but a distinct tool with a different reliability profile, drawing on production use across a bootstrapped software business rather than benchmark claims or airplane-coding anecdotes. An RTX 6000 Pro paid for itself within two or three months, but the post is candid about Qwen's worst failure modes: infinite loops and hallucination that worsen once you quantize down to fit a consumer GPU.

title: Inside Claude Managed Agents: Reverse Engineering the Security Boundaries of Anthropic's Hosted Agent Runtime
blurb: Pluto Security reverse-engineered Claude Managed Agents, Anthropic's hosted cloud runtime, finding gVisor isolation with a three-layer egress system that blocks direct DNS and outbound TCP, plus a vault credential proxy that keeps secrets out of the sandbox to structurally prevent prompt-injection theft. The default config is maximally permissive, and even in limited-networking mode the egress allowlist is silently expanded with six extra Anthropic hosts, including sentry.io and a staging endpoint.

title: GlassWASM: WebAssembly Malware Found in Trojanized Open VSX Extensions
blurb: Socket's Threat Research team found WebAssembly malware embedded in trojanized VS Code extensions on the Open VSX registry, compiled from Go with TinyGo and auto-executed on activation via a renamed loader. The payload encrypts every string with ChaCha20 and uses the Solana blockchain as a takedown-resistant C2 dead-drop, polling an attacker wallet's on-chain memo field to fetch and run OS-specific second-stage commands through Node's child_process.

title: How We Cut Semgrep's Taint Analysis Time by 75%
blurb: Semgrep rearchitected its interfile taint analysis to cut runtime by 75%, eliminating the "run taint twice" approach that recomputed taint configs and re-ran dataflow over every function after already producing cross-file taint signatures. The post details how passing signatures back into the original intrafile engine duplicated most of the analysis work, and how unifying the two paths removed the redundant pass without changing detection results.

title: Give GitHub Copilot CLI real code intelligence with language servers
blurb: This guide explains how to wire Language Server Protocol servers into GitHub Copilot CLI so the agent resolves symbols through go-to-definition, find-references, and type resolution instead of grepping through JARs, site-packages, and node_modules. The LSP Setup skill automates installation and configuration across 14 languages, giving the agent precise types, overloads, and signatures that text-search heuristics miss.

title: Android 17 Expands AI Agent Integration
blurb: Android 17 expands AppFunctions, a platform API that lets apps register capabilities as orchestratable tools for Android MCP, an on-device equivalent of the Model Context Protocol that Gemini and other agents can discover and execute against an app's local state. The alpha Jetpack library exposes a function as an agent tool by annotating a class and documenting it with KDoc, and the release ships Android 17 source to AOSP alongside mandatory large-screen resizability.

title: LLMs Are Complicated Now
blurb: The author argues that LLM architectures have lost their early simplicity, evolving from clean stacks of repeated Transformer modules into tangles of attention variants, mixture-of-experts routing, mixed-in vision and audio encoders, and inference-time sharding across GPUs. Drawing a parallel to how recommendation systems grew complex, they warn that performance optimization is becoming load-bearing, making it hard to keep a pure baseline definition that agents could reliably fuse into optimized kernels.

title: Building a design system specced for engineers and agents
blurb: Evil Martians built a design system for Currents, a Cypress and Playwright test observability platform, in seven weeks by running an AI-assisted UI audit and shipping standards that engineers and coding agents can both consume without a designer in the loop. The post details making the system AI-readable so tools like Cursor and Claude stop spawning a new component on every PR, trading ad-hoc design decisions for a foundation that keeps generated UI consistent.

title: Four Ways We're Using Our MCP Server at Figma
blurb: Figma expanded its MCP server to push design decisions into the tools where code is written, with new support for custom fonts, MCP skills, and architecture layouts in FigJam. The post details four internal workflows, including an agent that refreshes a living Figma Slides deck by pulling context from Slack, Google Drive, and release notes, handling roughly the first 80% of the content work before human review.

title: Ramp SWE-Bench: a private, production-grounded coding benchmark
blurb: Ramp released Ramp SWE-Bench, a private coding benchmark built from real engineering problems faced in its production codebase.

title: Announcing Stack Overflow for Agents
blurb: Stack Overflow released Stack Overflow for Agents, an API-first knowledge exchange that lets coding agents query and contribute real-time, peer-validated solutions instead of each rediscovering the same fixes in isolation. The beta targets what the team calls the Ephemeral Intelligence Gap, where an agent's hard-won context vanishes when its session ends, by giving agents a shared store of patterns, bug fixes, and current library and API usage.

title: A 27-Year-Old Authentication Bypass in OpenBSD's PPP Stack
blurb: OpenBSD's PAP authenticator used attacker-controlled length fields as the bcmp comparison length, so zero-length credentials bypassed authentication entirely for 27 years.

title: Write cleaner and faster Python code
blurb: InfoWorld's Python roundup covers Meta's Pyrefly 1.0 type-checking linter, which ships with PyTorch and Django support, and Python 3.15's new sampling profiler for low-overhead bottleneck hunting. It also walks through virtual environment tooling and flags Hades, a supply-chain malware strain that hides in PyPI packages and evades LLM-based code analysis.

title: Your browser tab could become encrypted storage for someone else’s files
blurb: Safecloud, a system from IENYC professor Gregory Magarshak, builds decentralized encrypted storage where browser tabs act as "Drop" nodes holding ciphertext chunks in IndexedDB while routing servers hold no keys. Files are split into fixed-size chunks encrypted client-side, with chunk addresses computed over encrypted bytes so storage nodes can verify content without decrypting it, and a single key-derivation hierarchy that also enables one-step segment seeking for streaming media.

title: The Thundering Herd Problem in Agentic AI: Why Traditional Fixes Fall Short
blurb: This CockroachDB post argues that the thundering herd problem changes shape in agentic AI, because the synchronized load is generated internally by intentional fan-out and parallelization rather than by an external trigger you can trace. The author identifies a non-linear saturation point between 700 and 1,000 concurrent agents where behavior shifts sharply, meaning load tests at 500 agents predict almost nothing about 5,000 and most teams hit the wall in production.

title: Show HN: Talos – Open-source WASM interpreter for Lean
blurb: Talos is an open-source WebAssembly interpreter written in Lean 4, where the same definitions that execute a Wasm program are the ones you prove theorems about, eliminating the drift between a spec interpreter and the implementation. Proofs are built on weakest-precondition calculus for compositional reasoning over loops and branches, with the trade-off being clarity over speed: the work-in-progress project optimizes for verifiable semantics rather than execution performance.

title: CS 6120: Advanced Compilers: The Self-Guided Online Course (2020)
blurb: Cornell's PhD-level Advanced Compilers course, free and self-guided, covering IRs, dataflow, SSA, LLVM, and GC through papers and hacking tasks.

title: The golden rule of Customizable Select
blurb: Customizable select arrives in Safari 27, letting developers fully style `<select>` elements with custom arrows, icons, and option layouts without JavaScript libraries or nested `<div>` hacks, while keeping built-in keyboard navigation and accessibility semantics. The post's core rule is to always give every `<option>` real text content or an accessible label, since icon-only options break screen readers, assistive output, and any browser that doesn't yet support the control.

title: Introducing the MDN MCP server
blurb: Mozilla released the MDN MCP server, which exposes MDN's web platform documentation and browser compatibility data to any MCP-compatible client, including VS Code, Zed, Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex CLI. The server targets the staleness problem in coding agents, letting them check current Baseline support for features like the light-dark() CSS function instead of relying on training-cutoff knowledge, with the trade-off that it ships as experimental.

title: Malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins steal AI API keys from developers
blurb: Aikido Security found at least 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins, published under seven vendor accounts and installed close to 70,000 times, that exfiltrate the OpenAI, DeepSeek, and SiliconFlow API keys developers enter into their settings to a hardcoded server. The plugins, first published in October 2025 with new ones added as recently as June 2026, function as advertised while quietly stealing credentials on "Apply" and reselling them to paid users.

title: Vercel Connect
blurb: Vercel released Connect, now in public beta, which replaces long-lived provider tokens with runtime credential exchange for AI agents. You register a connector once for a provider like Slack or GitHub, then the @vercel/connect SDK hands your app a short-lived, task-scoped token on demand, so no permanent secret lives in your environment and a leak exposes far less than a never-expiring token would.

title: AWS announces AWS Blocks, an open-source framework for composing application backends on AWS (Preview)
blurb: AWS released AWS Blocks in public preview, an open-source TypeScript framework that lets application developers compose backends with database tables, authentication, real-time messaging, AI agents, and background jobs without touching infrastructure tools directly. The same code runs against a local Postgres-backed environment with no AWS account and deploys to production AWS services with zero changes, with end-to-end type safety from schema to frontend and an escape hatch into AWS CDK for direct resource configuration.

title: Angular Signals in practice: Building a signal-first form in Angular
blurb: Angular's Signal Forms API expresses form behavior as state and derivation rather than event orchestration, and this walkthrough builds a registration form (email, password, confirmation, terms acceptance) to show how validation, interaction state, and submission logic change in practice. The author argues much of the coordination logic disappears when forms are declarative, while flagging that teams should weigh the new trade-offs before adopting the approach broadly.

title: $10,000,000 on the line: how we measure Devin's engineering output
blurb: Cognition's Ryan Bai argues that Devin's value should be measured by a per-customer guarantee, with Cognition putting up to $10M behind the claim that Devin ships more engineering output than it costs. The post details the measurement system built to validate this on independent data, framing AI coding agents as a usage-based output bet rather than a per-seat subscription.

title: I Stored a Website in a Favicon
blurb: A developer encoded an entire HTML page into a favicon's RGB pixels, fitting a 212-byte payload into 71 pixels decoded client-side.

title: Migrating from GNU Stow to Chezmoi
blurb: Developer migrates dotfiles from GNU Stow to chezmoi, swapping symlinks for a managed source repo to keep three Macs converged.

title: Replit is now available in Claude
blurb: Replit is now available directly inside Claude, letting you design an app in Claude Design with natural language and send it to Replit to keep building, refining, and shipping in one flow. Claude can also hand off any general development task to Replit through the official Replit Connector, so spinning up a backend or iterating on an existing project no longer requires copy-pasting between tools or switching context.

title: Claude Code now supports artifacts
blurb: Claude Code can now publish its work as artifacts, live interactive web pages built from the full session context including your codebase, connectors, and conversation. The pages cover use cases like PR walkthroughs, dashboards, incident timelines, and release checklists, refreshing in place as the session progresses, with every publish saved as a new version at the same link plus full version history.

title: The Enter key that quietly breaks Japanese input (and how to test if your app does it)
blurb: The author explains a bug class that breaks Japanese text entry: apps that treat the Enter key as submit will hijack the IME confirmation press users rely on to accept a romaji-to-kanji conversion, silently breaking typing for Japanese users in NocoDB, Chatwoot, and similar tools. The fix is checking event.isComposing (or keyCode === 229) to ignore Enter mid-composition, and the post includes a two-minute test to detect the issue in your own forms.

title: pg_kpart version 1.0
blurb: pg_kpart 1.0 is a PostgreSQL extension that blocks queries scanning every partition without a usable partition-key predicate, preventing accidental full-hierarchy scans.

title: The Mythical Agent-Month
blurb: The author revisits Fred Brooks's The Mythical Man-Month to ask whether a single developer orchestrating a swarm of AI coding agents escapes the coordination overhead, scope creep, and architectural drift that Brooks's Law pinned on large teams. He argues the human expert remains essential for now, supplying conceptual integrity and continuous supervision, but expects the same old bottlenecks to resurface once one person is managing many parallel agent sessions.

title: How we built an internal data analytics agent
blurb: GitHub built Qubot, a Copilot-powered analytics agent that lets any employee query the company's data warehouse in plain language and get an answer in seconds. The architecture splits into three parts: a UI layer (Slack, VS Code, Copilot CLI), a context layer that encodes which data model, grain, and filters to use, and a query engine. Every answer is saved as a markdown report in a pull request, so teams can refine the query or promote it to a dashboard.

title: Datasette Apps: Host custom HTML applications inside Datasette
blurb: Datasette Apps, a new plugin, lets you host self-contained HTML+JavaScript applications inside Datasette that run in a sandboxed iframe and query your data with read-only SQL, or write queries when configured with stored queries. The sandbox blocks cookie and localStorage access and injects a CSP header that prevents outbound HTTP requests, so a buggy or malicious app cannot exfiltrate private data.

title: Caching for Dollars, Not Hits: An Exact Offline Reference for Cloud-Egress Caching and the Crossover That Decides When It Pays
blurb: This paper reframes cache design around dollars instead of hit rate, arguing that when misses fetch from cloud object storage, the bill is per-GET and per-byte egress, so a rarely fetched but expensive object can cost thousands of times more than a frequent cheap one. The authors build an exact offline dollar-optimum benchmark and show cost-aware GreedyDual cuts LRU's regret to roughly a tenth. A closed-form crossover, GET_fee/egress_rate (about 4KB on S3, 330B on GCS), predicts which deployments actually need dollar-aware caching.

title: Amazon S3 annotations: attach rich, queryable context directly to your objects
blurb: Amazon S3 added annotations, letting you attach up to 1,000 named, queryable annotations per object (1 MB each, 1 GB total) in JSON, XML, YAML, or plain text, editable without rewriting the object. Enabling S3 Metadata flows annotations into managed annotation tables queryable from Amazon Athena, giving teams a way to keep evolving context like transcripts or AI summaries alongside petabyte-scale data without separate metadata databases.

title: Query-aware Routing for Filtered Approximate Nearest Neighbors Search
blurb: Filtered ANN search pairs vector similarity with attribute predicates, and this paper benchmarks every major categorical method across datasets and three predicate types, finding no single winner even within one dataset. The authors propose query-aware routing: a lightweight model predicts each method's recall per query, then a router consults an offline recall/QPS table to pick the best trade-off. Trained on six datasets and tested on five unseen ones, it leads existing baselines with negligible latency overhead.

title: Source-Grounded Data Generation for Text-to-JSON Learning
blurb: STAGE is a synthetic-data pipeline for training models to extract structured JSON from long unstructured documents like financial filings and clinical records. It uses LLMs to synthesize reports and JSON schemas at scale while validating every ground-truth value against the underlying spreadsheet, so the training data stays accurate. Fine-tuning Qwen3-4B on STAGE data lifted exact match from 31.37% to 74.27% and value accuracy from 45.46% to 90.69% on the authors' 851-example benchmark.

title: Databricks lets a Genie One AI agent coworker out of its magic lamp
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One, an agentic coworker built on Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that builds a knowledge graph across Databricks plus 50-plus connected apps including Slack, Jira, Confluence, and Google Drive. The system ranks enterprise data assets with an OntoRank scheme modeled on PageRank, giving multiple agents a shared set of metric definitions and master data so answers stay consistent across the org.

title: Databricks targets AI operations bottlenecks with ZeroOps
blurb: Databricks released Genie ZeroOps, an agentic operations capability in private preview that automates monitoring, root-cause analysis, and remediation across data and AI workloads, using Unity Catalog lineage to trace anomalies and test proposed fixes in isolation before human review. The pitch targets the maintenance tax that now consumes most data-team time, shifting engineers from firefighting pipeline and model failures to approving AI-generated fixes.

title: Databricks expands Lakehouse to unify OLAP and OLTP
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP (Lake Transactional/Analytical Processing) at its Data + AI Summit, combining Lakebase serverless Postgres with the Lakehouse under one Unity Catalog governance model so operational data is queryable for analytics with no pipelines. LTAP ends the separate copying between transactional and analytical stores, keeping data in open Iceberg and Delta formats while letting OLTP and OLAP workloads scale independently with strict isolation.

title: The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud
blurb:
This paper argues database systems should stop treating the network as a black box and co-design it with database operations. The proposed bi-channel paradigm splits communication into a high-performance user-space UDP data path for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive work and a kernel TCP control path for coordination and recovery, exploiting modern NICs without reimplementing reliability. The authors saturate 200 Gbit/s on a distributed shuffle with three CPU cores and push millions of messages per second through a replicated key-value store.

(83 words)

title: AgenticDB: Agentic Performance Reconfiguration for Database Workloads
blurb: AgenticDB reframes database tuning as an agentic reconfiguration loop rather than iterative knob search, proposing both DBMS- and OS-level changes, applying them under safety constraints, and using runtime feedback to diagnose bottlenecks and guide later decisions. Tested on MySQL and PostgreSQL across YCSB, Sysbench, and TPC-H, it beat the strongest baseline by 118.1% on average while cutting time-to-best by 22.6%. The gains came from the OS-level action space, a robust execution lifecycle, and memory that carries experience across tasks.

title: Policy-aware Vector Search: A Vision for Fine Grained Access Control in Vector Databases
blurb: This paper proposes fine-grained access control for vector databases, formalizing a policy model and enforcement problem for the RAG and organizational AI pipelines that increasingly query embeddings in security-sensitive contexts. The authors show the core tension: enforcing per-user policies correctly while keeping ANN recall high and query latency low, since vector search mixes structured filters with approximate semantic results in ways relational FGAC patterns do not handle. The work compares enforcement strategies and lays out open problems rather than shipping a finished system.

title: Vibe Coding for Visualization Implementation: An Empirical Study of Practices and Challenges
blurb: This paper studies how people actually use AI "vibe coding" tools to build data visualizations, running 16 participants through two viz tasks plus interviews to map their prompting, evaluation, and iteration habits. The finding that matters for data teams: visualization is a worse fit for natural-language generation than general coding, because charts demand precise intent-to-pixel alignment that users struggle to specify and verify, leaving them stuck in iteration loops when the output looks plausible but encodes the data wrong.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
blurb: This paper introduces the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a metric that measures how well a predictive model respects predefined logical constraints, independent of its accuracy. RVS handles hard and soft rules separately, works on any model over a relational vocabulary, and compiles to auto-generated SQL queries for Horn rules. The authors show that two models with comparable accuracy can diverge sharply in logical compliance, a gap standard metrics miss and a real concern for healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems.

title: Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset
blurb: GitHub published a repository-level dataset under CC0-1.0 that maps multilingual developer content across READMEs, issues, and pull requests, giving teams a discovery layer for non-English code documentation. The release targets researchers and developers training multilingual AI, surfacing where natural-language signals live inside repositories rather than treating code corpora as English-only.

title: Snowflake and the Agentic Resource Discovery Specification
blurb: Snowflake announced support for the Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD) Specification, an open protocol developed with Microsoft, GoDaddy, and others that standardizes how AI agents, MCP servers, and API tools are cataloged and discovered across an enterprise. ARD adds a federated discovery layer above existing invocation protocols, so an agent a data team ships in Snowflake becomes findable from Copilot or any AI client without manual wiring, while the discovery service stays out of the invocation path.

title: DeQL: A Decision Query Language for Prescriptive Analytics over Relational Data
blurb: DeQL extends SQL to express decision queries, adding two constructs: CREATE CANDIDATES, which defines the option space from relational sources, and DECIDE, which declares decision variables, named constraints, and an objective. The user states what to optimize and the engine picks the solver, keeping the structure of subset selection, allocation, scheduling, and assignment problems visible inside the warehouse. This first specification fixes the core constructs and adds extensions for optimization under uncertainty, inline model scoring, and time-bounded solving.

title: Ingesting the Milky Way: Petabyte-Scale with Zerobus Ingest
blurb: Databricks released Zerobus Ingest, a fully managed serverless streaming service that pushes data directly into Delta tables governed by Unity Catalog, removing the need to run Kafka when the destination is the lakehouse. In benchmarking against NASA's NEOWISE dataset, it ingested 1PB in under 24 hours at a sustained 12 GB/s to a single table with zero pre-configuration.

title: When failover isn't safe: Building high-availability PostgreSQL on Kubernetes
blurb: Datadog rearchitected its Kubernetes-based PostgreSQL clusters to use Patroni-coordinated synchronous replication after a gameday exposed an unsafe failover mode. A simulated zonal failure spiked network latency, stalling replication so far that no replica was current enough to promote without data loss, leaving clusters stuck waiting for latency to subside. The writeup covers how they traded durability against latency and validated the design through benchmarking and failure testing.

title: The only scalable delete in Postgres is DROP TABLE
blurb: PlanetScale argues that the only Postgres delete that actually scales is dropping or truncating an entire table, because MVCC makes large DELETEs "work added" rather than work done. Big batch deletes leave dead tuples behind, don't return disk to the OS until a lock-heavy VACUUM FULL, skip index cleanup, and add write and replication load that can stall other writers. The takeaway is a schema-design one: partition or shard so bulk cleanup becomes a DROP TABLE or TRUNCATE instead of a DELETE.

title: British Columbia, Time Zones, and Postgres
blurb: British Columbia's switch to permanent UTC-7 can silently shift Postgres timestamptz appointments by an hour unless tzdata is updated.

title: Introducing the Open Knowledge Format
blurb: Google introduced the Open Knowledge Format, an open spec representing AI context as markdown files with YAML frontmatter.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic overhauled Claude Design, adding design-system imports from GitHub repos or design files, code round-trips, and a rebuilt editor that cuts the heavy token consumption that let early users exhaust a weekly Claude Pro allowance in under half an hour. The update repositions the tool from a prototyping demo into a brand-compliance layer that checks generated UI against imported components and auto-corrects before export.

title: Modos Color Monitor Pushes E-Paper Displays Further
blurb: Modos launched a Crowd Supply campaign for Flow, a 13.3-inch color e-paper monitor at 3,200x2,400 with touch and a 60-Hz refresh.

title: Snap Specs bring rich augmented reality to smart glasses
blurb: Snap released the Specs, standalone AR glasses that combine the immersive display of a mixed-reality headset with the lightweight frame of consumer AI glasses, shown this week at the Augmented World Expo. The product targets a middle ground between bulky passthrough headsets and display-less audio glasses, betting that designers and developers will build for a form factor light enough to wear in public.

title: Adobe embeds agentic AI workflows across Creative Cloud, shifting from media generation to production orchestration
blurb: Adobe moved its Creative Agent into public beta across Premiere Pro, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, repositioning its AI from media generator to an orchestration layer that reads natural-language prompts and drives each app's APIs to run multi-step production tasks. The upgraded Firefly studio adds Elements, a visual variables library for reusing characters and objects across generations, and Projects, a persistent memory layer that retains assets and session context.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, best known for its text-to-image AI, announced Midjourney Medical and its first hardware product: a scanner that submerges you in water and uses a ring of half a million ultrasonic emitters to build a sub-millimeter 3D body map in under 60 seconds. Built with Butterfly Network under an exclusive ultrasound-on-chip license, the machine targets MRI-quality imaging at roughly 100x the speed, with company-run scanning spas planned over the next 12 months.

title: Adobe expands Firefly capabilities, extends agentic tools to Creative Cloud apps
blurb: Adobe expanded Firefly with pre-built Creative Skills, including a Brand Kit creation flow that generates logos, palettes, fonts, and social-channel promo videos from a single prompt, plus new Elements and Projects features for reusing characters and organizing assets. The company also added agentic Firefly sidebars to Premiere, Photoshop, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, letting users delegate tasks like renaming assets, fact-checking against briefs, and applying brand-guideline updates.

title: Heatherwick Studio unveils "space observatory" in AlUla
blurb: Heatherwick Studio released designs for AlUla Manara, a textured-stone stargazing and research center planned for the Saudi desert near the AlUla UNESCO World Heritage Site. The building is shaped around the region's exceptional night-sky visibility, pairing a public visitor experience with astronomical research facilities in one of the few remaining low-light-pollution sites accessible to a mass audience.

title: How a fine-art obsession helped this fantasy indie game attract millions of views
blurb: Sovereign Tower, a solo-built indie RPG from Wild Wits, swaps open-world scale for a single hand-painted tower and an illustration-led art direction that draws on classical fairy-tale etchings and Ivan Bilibin to give every character instant personality. Art director Gobert's advice is the takeaway: nail your project's identity first, keep every asset coherent within one style, and limit your palette to build a strong visual signature.

title: How&How gives Brunel's SS Great Britain a second life as Bristol Dockyards
blurb: How&How rebranded Brunel's SS Great Britain as the Bristol Dockyards, expanding the single ship into a three-part cultural destination spanning the Being Brunel museum, the Brunel Institute archive, and the vessel itself. The identity leans on a collage system that layers 200 years of timelines, typography, and dockyard ephemera, plus a defiantly un-nautical pink pulled from Totterdown's terraced houses to counter slipping ticket sales and an ageing audience.

title: ZHA designs waterfront performance venue in China to "crescendo and flare"
blurb: Zaha Hadid Architects completed the Songshan Lake Exhibition and Performance Center, a waterfront complex of three interconnected volumes whose curved forms reference traditional Cantonese opera costumes. Sited in Guangdong's Greater Bay Area, the building anchors an undulating 1,200-seat Grand Theatre alongside exhibition space, extending ZHA's signature parametric language into a civic cultural venue for south China.

title: Bear app developers announce Lettera, a beautiful Markdown editor for Mac
blurb: The developers behind Bear released Lettera, a standalone native macOS Markdown editor in beta on TestFlight, built on the same WYSIWYG editor that powers Bear 2 with live CommonMark rendering, hidden syntax while writing, tables, code blocks, and Math via MathJax. It opens individual files or entire folders as workspaces, defaults to an iCloud folder, and exports to plain text, RTF, HTML, PDF, ePub, and more.

title: Vibe Coding for Visualization Implementation: An Empirical Study of Practices and Challenges
blurb: A study of 16 participants using AI "vibe coding" tools to build data visualizations found that natural-language generation lowers the entry barrier but struggles to align user intent with precise visual output. The authors map how practitioners prompt, evaluate, and iterate, and document where the loop breaks down when a chart needs exact encoding rather than an approximate result.

title: The 2026 Core77 Design Awards Toys & Play Winners
blurb: The 2026 Core77 Design Awards named Geni, an AI storytelling toy that lets children build narratives from physical character-and-action blocks, the Professional winner in Toys & Play, with student honors going to ODI, an assistive device for children with psycho-sensory disabilities in physical education. Jury lead Irene Guerrieri said selections prioritized quality of human interaction and inclusivity, weighing play as a fundamental part of life at every age.

title: Gaudí's "ghost of an alternative New York" visualised by AI artist Thierry Lechanteur
blurb: Belgian AI artist Thierry Lechanteur rendered Gaudí's unbuilt Hotel Attraction, a supertall New York tower designed around 1908, as a series of digital visuals released for Dezeen's Gaudí Centenary series. The renders reconstruct one of Gaudí's least-known speculative projects from sparse archival sketches, offering a concrete look at a building that was never realized rather than a faithful historical record.

title: Apple Music in iOS 27 introduces new design changes in two key areas
blurb: Apple Music's iOS 27 update redesigns artist pages so the artist image bleeds into the content below and its colors drive the full page palette, with info, play, and star buttons pulled front and center under the name. Album pages get a parallel refresh that hasn't hit beta yet, and the systemwide Liquid Glass refinements add a slider for dialing in your preferred look.

title: CSSQuake
blurb: A playable Quake clone rendered entirely in CSS, with multiplayer, dynamic lighting, and particles.

title: The golden rule of Customizable Select
blurb: Customizable Select arrives in Safari 27, letting developers fully style native `<select>` elements (custom arrows, icons, color swatches, option layouts) without JavaScript libraries or nested `<div>` hacks, while keeping built-in keyboard navigation and accessibility. WebKit's one rule: always give option elements real text content or accessible labels, since icon-only options break screen readers, scannability, and unsupported browsers all at once.

title: Building a design system specced for engineers and agents
blurb: Evil Martians documented how they built a design system for Currents, a Cypress and Playwright test observability tool, specced so engineers and AI coding agents can apply it without a designer in the loop. The seven-week case study is a useful read on making design tokens and component rules AI-readable, so every Cursor- or Claude-generated PR converges on the system instead of spawning one-off components.

title: AMD silently removes memory encryption from consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving users unaware that they may be vulnerable — security feature vanishes after newer AGESA firmware, AMD engineers go radio silent when pressed about the change
blurb: AMD appears to have silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen CPUs outside its Pro lineup, stripping protection against physical attacks that siphon data from connected memory, with the change traced to AGESA 1.2.7.0 firmware. The removal is undetectable on Windows and hard to spot on Linux, so defenders relying on at-rest memory encryption for consumer endpoints should verify TSME status directly rather than assume it is still active.

title: CISA warns Fortinet users to secure devices after FortiBleed leak
blurb: CISA warned Fortinet customers to secure their appliances after the "FortiBleed" leak exposed plaintext VPN and admin credentials for 73,932 FortiGate firewalls across 21,632 domains and 194 countries, with threat actors already using the data to target internet-facing devices. Affected owners should terminate all SSL VPN and admin sessions, reset VPN and admin passwords, enable phishing-resistant MFA, and review logs for lateral movement.

title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
blurb: Researchers from multiple security firms linked the Popa botnet, which has hijacked millions of Android TV boxes into a residential-proxy network for the past four years, to NetNut, a proxy provider run by publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies (NASDAQ: ALAR). Popa operates as a plugin of the Vo1d malware campaign, scattering ad fraud, account takeovers, and data-scraping traffic across more than 1.4 million IP addresses.

title: Splunk Enterprise Vulnerability Exploited in Attacks Days After Disclosure
blurb: Splunk confirmed limited in-the-wild exploitation of CVE-2026-20253, a critical unauthenticated flaw in Splunk Enterprise where the PostgreSQL sidecar service endpoint lacks authentication controls, allowing any network-reachable attacker to create or truncate arbitrary files and achieve RCE. Patches landed June 10 for versions 10.2 before 10.2.4 and 10.0 before 10.0.7; CISA added it to KEV on June 18 with a June 21 federal remediation deadline.

title: Unpatchable 'usbliter8' Exploit Breaks Apple A12 and A13 SecureROM Boot Chain
blurb: Security researchers at Paradigm Shift published usbliter8, an unpatchable SecureROM exploit achieving arbitrary code execution on Apple's A12 and A13 chips via a buffer underflow in the Synopsys DWC2 USB controller, abusing the DART IOMMU's bypass mode to overwrite SRAM. The flaw is burned into silicon and requires physical DFU-mode access over USB, so treat lost or seized iPhone XS through 11-series and SE devices as fully compromisable below the signed boot chain.

title: AutoJack Attack Lets One Web Page Hijack AI Agent for Host Code Execution
blurb: Microsoft researchers detailed AutoJack, an exploit chain that turns an AI browsing agent into a remote-code-execution vector by chaining three flaws in AutoGen Studio's MCP WebSocket: localhost trust, skipped authentication, and an unallowlisted command parameter. A single attacker page loaded by the agent, via planted link or prompt injection, runs arbitrary host commands unauthenticated; the vulnerable handler shipped only in pre-releases 0.4.3.dev1 and 0.4.3.dev2, with the fix in GitHub main at commit b047730.

title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
blurb: F5 patched two critical RCE flaws in NGINX Open Source, CVE-2026-42530 and CVE-2026-42055 (both CVSS 9.2), each exploitable by remote unauthenticated attackers against the HTTP/3 QUIC module and HTTP/2 proxy modules respectively. Code execution requires ASLR to be disabled or bypassed, but admins who can't upgrade immediately should disable HTTP/3 and remove the ignore_invalid_headers off directive as interim mitigations.

title: Embedding Forbidden Text in Spyware to Discourage AI Analysis
blurb: A malware developer is embedding fake system instructions and policy-triggering text about nuclear and biological weapons into a JavaScript comment block at the top of their spyware, designed to derail LLM-based triage that feeds file headers to a model without isolating them as untrusted data. The trick only affects naive LLM-first pipelines and can cause refusals or premature classification; YARA, entropy checks, AST parsing, and behavioral rules still detect the payload after the comment.

title: Users cry foul after AMD stripped memory crypto from its consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from its consumer Ryzen CPUs, leaving DRAM contents unencrypted and exposing systems to cold-boot and physical memory-extraction attacks that the feature was meant to mitigate. The change was undocumented and appears deliberate, so defenders relying on at-rest memory encryption for laptops or kiosks should verify TSME support per SKU rather than assume parity with prior generations.

title: The Gentlemen RaaS Uses GentleKiller EDR Framework Targeting 400 Security Processes
blurb: The Gentlemen RaaS operates GentleKiller, a mature EDR-killer framework shipped to affiliates that hunts 400 processes tied to 48 security products, using BYOVD across eight variants that each abuse a different vulnerable driver (Kaspersky, FACEIT, Valorant) and impersonate legitimate vendors via spoofed certificates and icons. ESET notes the group operationalizes newly disclosed BYOVD PoCs within days of release, so defenders should enforce driver blocklists and monitor for signed-but-anomalous driver loads ahead of encryptor deployment.

title: Cybersecurity Firms Impacted by Klue Supply Chain Attack
blurb: A supply chain attack on market intelligence platform Klue, beginning June 11, let attackers push a malicious code update that harvested customer OAuth tokens and abused the Salesforce REST API to exfiltrate CRM data. Huntress and Recorded Future confirmed impact limited to business contact and sales data; Klue revoked all OAuth tokens and Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards app on June 17.

title: FortiBleed campaign exposes 75,000 Fortinet firewalls worldwide
blurb: The FortiBleed campaign exposed working administrator and SSL VPN credentials for roughly 75,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls across 194 countries, about half of all Fortinet firewalls visible on Shodan. Researchers including Kevin Beaumont and Hudson Rock attribute the harvest to credentials recovered from stolen configuration files over time, so patching alone is insufficient; defenders should rotate all FortiGate admin and VPN credentials and audit configs for prior compromise.

title: Build your own vulnerability harness
blurb: Cloudflare details how it built a model-agnostic vulnerability harness for Project Glasswing, deliberately swapping models across the pipeline so one model handles discovery and a different one validates, cross-checking findings through distinct reasoning. The architecture persists hundreds of investigations across runs, traces vulnerabilities through cross-repo dependencies, and filters thousands of raw candidates down to a triaged queue of actionable fixes.

title: MosaicLeaks: Can your research agent keep a secret?
blurb: MosaicLeaks introduces a deep-research benchmark showing that agents combining private documents with web retrieval leak sensitive enterprise data through their outbound query logs, where an observer reassembles fragments via the mosaic effect without ever seeing the documents. The authors found leakage is common across tested models and that training purely for task accuracy worsens it. Their privacy-aware RL method, PA-DR, cut answer and full-information leakage from 34.0% to 9.9% while raising strict chain success from 48.7% to 58.7%.

title: Salesforce Disables Klue App Integration After OAuth Token Abuse Exposes Customer Data
blurb: Salesforce disabled the Klue Battlecards app integration after the Icarus extortion group compromised a legacy integration credential at Klue, harvested OAuth tokens via a malicious code update, and accessed customer Salesforce data including business contacts and price quotes from victims like Huntress. The breach stemmed from Klue's connection rather than a Salesforce platform flaw, so defenders running Klue or similar third-party integrations should revoke and rotate affected OAuth tokens and audit connected-app access for anomalous activity.

title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
blurb: Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service maintains a suite of EDR killers, led by the custom GentleKiller tool, which uses bring-your-own-vulnerable-driver (BYOVD) to gain kernel privileges and disable security engines, per ESET. GentleKiller spans at least eight variants targeting 400-plus processes across roughly 48 vendors including CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, and Microsoft, and its modular design lets affiliates swap drivers to weaponize newly disclosed flaws without major code changes.

title: DragonForce Hackers Abuse Microsoft Teams Relays to Hide Backdoor.Turn C2 Traffic
blurb: DragonForce ransomware operators deployed Backdoor.Turn, a custom Go RAT that hides C2 traffic by acquiring an anonymous Teams visitor token, routing through a legitimate Microsoft TURN relay, then running a QUIC session to the attacker's server. Defenders saw only outbound connections to Microsoft Teams during the one-to-two-month dwell; watch for DLL side-loading, BYOVD driver abuse (including a Huawei audio driver), and injection into DbgView64.exe.

title: Researchers drop checkm8-style BootROM exploit for A12 and A13 iPhones
blurb: Researchers released a checkm8-style BootROM exploit affecting A12 and A13 iPhones, including the iPhone XS, XR, and 11 lines, that enables tethered jailbreaks and physical-access compromise. Because the flaw sits in read-only boot code, Apple cannot patch it in software, so defenders managing affected devices should treat them as vulnerable to data extraction once an attacker has physical possession.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft disclosed Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via .lnk files on USB drives and monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating stolen credentials and timed screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. By bundling a portable Tor client and IP-less C2 with remote code execution, the malware doubles as a lightweight backdoor, so defenders should restrict USB autorun, block unauthorized Tor traffic, and monitor for clipboard-hijacking behavior.

title: 7,000 Langflow servers are under attack. LangGraph and LangChain have the same holes
blurb: A path traversal in Langflow's file upload endpoint is under active in-the-wild exploitation across roughly 7,000 exposed servers, yielding unauthenticated RCE per Tenable and VulnCheck. The same bug classes hit sibling frameworks: Check Point chained a SQLite-checkpointer SQL injection (CVE-2025-67644) to RCE in LangGraph, and Cyera found a LangChain-core prompt-loader traversal that reads secrets off disk.

title: 15,000 WordPress Websites Cleaned Up in SocGholish Botnet Takedown
blurb: Law enforcement in the Netherlands, Canada, the US, and Germany, working with Europol, dismantled the SocGholish (FakeUpdates) botnet, taking down 106 C&C servers and cleaning malware from 14,971 compromised WordPress sites. Operated by initial-access broker TA569 (UNC1543), the JavaScript dropper has seeded LockBit, RansomHub, and AsyncRAT via fake browser updates since 2017, so site owners should rotate credentials, enable MFA, and audit for leftover backdoors.

title: Klue OAuth breach victim list grows as Icarus hackers claim attack
blurb: Klue confirmed that attackers exploited a compromised legacy integration credential to steal OAuth tokens connecting its Battlecards platform to customer Salesforce environments, with the Icarus extortion group now claiming responsibility. Huntress and ReliaQuest observed the attackers generating tokens and running Python scripts against the Salesforce API to exfiltrate CRM data; organizations with Klue Salesforce integrations should revoke tokens and audit API query logs since June 12.

title: Microsoft found malware that hijacks crypto wallets and spreads through USB sticks
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a clipboard-hijacking worm it tracks as Trojan:Win32/CryptoBandits, spreading via malicious .lnk shortcuts on infected USB drives since February to steal crypto wallet seed phrases and private keys. It polls the Windows clipboard roughly every 500ms, exfiltrates captured keys and screenshots over Tor, and silently swaps copied recipient addresses for attacker-controlled ones; defenders should disable AutoRun, block .lnk execution on removable media, and restrict script hosts.

title: Authorities disrupt Evil Corp’s SocGholish botnet
blurb: A multinational law enforcement operation disrupted SocGholish (aka FakeUpdates), the Evil Corp-linked malware framework that has compromised mostly WordPress sites since 2017 to redirect users through traffic distribution systems and broker initial access for DoppelPaymer, LockBit, RansomHub, and other ransomware. Authorities from the US, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, and Europol seized 106 servers and remediated nearly 15,000 infected sites, and the FBI issued a PSA warning defenders about TDS-based intrusion chains.

title: NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models
blurb: This paper introduces a fully black-box probe that detects whether a text-to-image model has memorized a real person's likeness, requiring no reference photos, training-data access, or model internals. Tested on the new NAMESAKES dataset of over 1,000 public-figure names across fame levels, the probe reliably separates memorized identities from fabricated ones. For teams assessing T2I deployments, it offers a practical way to audit privacy exposure without white-box access to the model.

title: Apple is about to make Hide My Email useless
blurb: Apple will move Sign in with Apple and Hide My Email aliases to the @private.icloud.com subdomain, making relay addresses trivially distinguishable from regular iCloud mailboxes. The change lets services block all aliases without affecting real iCloud accounts, undermining the plausible deniability that made Hide My Email useful for privacy; existing @icloud.com aliases can still be generated until the switch lands.

title: Apple Patches Beats Studio Buds Flaw Letting Nearby Attackers Spy via Microphone
blurb: Apple released Beats Firmware Update 1B211 to patch CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an incorrect-authorization flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth audio SDK that lets an attacker within Bluetooth range pair with an unpaired Beats Studio Buds and listen through its microphone without user interaction. ERNW researchers disclosed the bug alongside two other Airoha SoC flaws at TROOPERS in June 2025, and Jabra shipped similar fixes in December.

title: USB worm spreads crypto-stealing malware via Windows shortcut files
blurb: Microsoft detailed a USB worm campaign, active since at least February, that spreads clipper malware via LNK shortcut files on removable drives, replacing copied crypto wallet addresses in the clipboard with attacker-controlled ones chosen to visually mimic the originals. The malware also harvests BIP39 seed phrases and private keys, captures screenshots, supports C2-triggered RCE, and exfiltrates over Tor, so defenders should hunt behaviorally and disable USB autorun and scheduled-task persistence rather than rely on signatures.

title: Microsoft Details Windows Clipper Malware Campaign Using USB LNK Worm and Tor-Based C2
blurb: Microsoft disclosed a Windows cryptocurrency clipper campaign active since February 2026 that spreads via malicious USB LNK files, intercepts clipboard data to swap crypto wallet addresses, and routes C2 traffic through a bundled Tor client over a local SOCKS5 proxy. The worm hides legitimate documents behind decoy shortcuts, persists via scheduled tasks, and exits if Task Manager is running, blending wallet theft with screenshot exfiltration and remote code execution.

title: Copilot searched your mailbox. LiteLLM handed out admin keys. Run this 5-check audit before your stack is next
blurb: Varonis disclosed SearchLeak (CVE-2026-42824), a zero-click exfiltration chain in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that lets a crafted microsoft.com URL inject prompts, bypass output sanitization via a rendering race, and route mailbox data out through a Bing SSRF. Four days earlier, Obsidian Security chained three LiteLLM flaws (CVSS 9.9) from a default low-privilege account to proxy admin and RCE; both vendors have patched.

title: Majority of Internet-Accessible REDCap Servers Outdated
blurb: Censys found that roughly 8,500 internet-exposed REDCap clinical-research servers are running outdated software, with just over 1% on the latest 17.1.3 release and 30% still on version 16.0.17. Google's GTIG reports the China-linked actor UNC6508 has targeted legacy REDCap instances since September 2023, deploying credential-harvesting malware and the InfiniteRed backdoor against US medical, academic, and military research orgs; operators should inventory deployments and patch to the current version.

title: Accenture to Acquire Majority Stake in Dragos, All of runZero, NetRise in $4.1 Billion OT Cybersecurity Push
blurb: Accenture announced it will acquire a majority stake in OT security firm Dragos and fully buy runZero and NetRise in a deal valued at roughly $4.1B, with runZero and NetRise to operate under an independent Dragos. The move expands Accenture's industrial cybersecurity reach and reads as a competitive response to ServiceNow's $7.75B acquisition of Armis.

title: GrapheneOS has been ported to Android 17 and official releases are coming soon
blurb: GrapheneOS has been fully ported to Android 17, with developer builds available now and the first official release scheduled for tomorrow across all supported Pixel devices, including the 6a, 7, 8, and 10 Pro Fold. The hardened mobile OS has been tested on each device ahead of public release, continuing its same-day tracking of upstream Android.

title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises +30K Fortinet Devices
blurb: A Russian-speaking threat group has compromised more than 30,791 internet-facing Fortinet firewalls and VPN gateways across 194 countries, with SOCRadar finding a verified repository of working credentials after discovering the attackers' exposed operational server. Researchers attribute this to credential compromise rather than a Fortinet flaw, with telecom, government, and critical infrastructure hardest hit, so defenders should rotate credentials on default, built-in, and long-lived admin accounts and enforce MFA on edge appliances.

title: Texas govt data breach exposes over 3 million driver’s licenses
blurb: The Texas Parks and Wildlife Department disclosed a breach at its third-party license-system vendor that exposed personal data for 3,087,721 hunting and fishing license customers, including driver's license details, passport numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, and home addresses. SSNs and financial data were not affected, but the exposed PII is sufficient for targeted phishing and social engineering, so impacted individuals should enroll in the offered credit monitoring and stay alert for impersonation scams.

title: When Lower Privileges Suffice: Investigating Over-Privileged Tool Selection in LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces ToolPrivBench, a benchmark measuring whether LLM agents pick higher-privilege tools when a sufficient lower-privilege alternative exists. Across eight domains and five risk patterns, the authors find over-privileged selection is common in mainstream agents and worsens after transient tool failures, and that general safety alignment and prompt-level guardrails don't reliably enforce least privilege. Their privilege-aware post-training defense substantially cut unnecessary high-privilege tool use while preserving capability, pointing to alignment rather than prompts as the durable control for agentic tool access.

title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
blurb: An ethical hacker known as BobDaHacker registered through FIFA's public Agent Platform and reached the same Microsoft Entra tenant backing FIFA's internal systems, gaining control over World Cup television streams and match management systems. The flaw was client-side authorization with no server-side enforcement: the frontend returned access-denied while the backend API served any authenticated user, so defenders should validate authorization on every API call, not just in the UI.

title: Apple patches high-severity eavesdropping vulnerability in Beats Studio Buds
blurb: Apple patched CVE-2025-20701 (CVSS 8.8), an improper-authentication flaw in the Airoha Bluetooth chip firmware of Beats Studio Buds that lets an attacker within radio range impersonate a previously paired device and eavesdrop through the phone's microphone. The fix ships in Beats Firmware Update 1B211, delivered automatically when the buds are paired with a nearby iPhone, iPad, or Mac; Jabra, Bose, and JBL have shipped the same Airoha SDK patch.

title: Cisco to Acquire WideField Security to Boost Splunk’s Agentic SOC
blurb: Cisco announced an agreement to acquire identity lifecycle security firm WideField Security to feed deeper identity and session intelligence into Splunk's Agentic SOC and Cisco's broader data fabric. Terms were not disclosed; this marks Cisco's third cybersecurity acquisition of 2026 after Galileo and Astrix, landing the same day Accenture revealed $4.1B in OT security deals for Dragos, runZero, and NetRise.

title: Accenture shells out $4.18B on three companies in big industrial cybersecurity push
